proposals.title: A Pharmacodynamic and Clinical Outcome Study to Inform Harmonized Susceptibility Breakpoints for Cefiderocol
proposals.motivation: As of 2025, cefiderocol, a critical siderophore cephalosporin for treating carbapenem-resistant Gram-negative infections, has discordant susceptibility breakpoints between the Clinical and Laboratory Standards Institute (CLSI), the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA), and the European Committee on Antimicrobial Susceptibility Testing (EUCAST). This discrepancy creates clinical confusion, complicates antimicrobial stewardship, and hinders the interpretation of global surveillance data, potentially impacting patient care and the utility of clinical trials.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a comprehensive analysis integrating pharmacokinetic/pharmacodynamic (PK/PD) targets, clinical outcome data from pivotal trials (e.g., CREDIBLE-CR, APEKS-NP), and contemporary molecular epidemiology of resistant isolates will identify a unified set of interpretive criteria that accurately predicts treatment success.
proposals.method: We will conduct a systematic analysis of patient-level microbiological and clinical outcome data from cefiderocol trials (Merck & Shionogi-sponsored) and real-world evidence. Using established PK/PD models (e.g., fT>MIC), we will correlate cefiderocol MICs with clinical and microbiological outcomes. Concurrently, we will employ long-read metagenomic sequencing (as in PI's prior work) on rectal swabs and bloodstream isolates to characterize the resistome (particularly β-lactamase genes like NDM, VIM, KPC) in isolates with MICs at critical breakpoint discrepancies. Statistical analyses, including classification and regression tree (CART) analysis and multivariable logistic regression, will define optimal breakpoints.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a robust, data-driven framework for harmonizing cefiderocol breakpoints across major standards organizations. It will directly support antimicrobial stewardship and clinical decision-making by resolving current interpretive conflicts. Furthermore, it will establish a methodological template for reconciling breakpoint discrepancies for other novel antimicrobials.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies have described breakpoint discordance, this proposal uniquely integrates advanced resistome characterization via long-read sequencing with detailed PK/PD and clinical outcome modeling specifically for cefiderocol, creating a multi-evidence approach to breakpoint harmonization that moves beyond epidemiological cutoff (ECOFF) analysis alone.

proposals.title: A High-Throughput Phenotypic Assay for Concurrent Measurement of Iron Acquisition and Antibiotic Susceptibility in Carbapenem-Resistant Enterobacterales
proposals.motivation: Iron is a critical nutrient for bacterial growth and virulence, and its acquisition systems are potential therapeutic targets. Pre-2026 research shows that iron availability can modulate antibiotic efficacy, but standard clinical microbiology tests (e.g., broth microdilution) do not assess this interaction. For multidrug-resistant pathogens like carbapenem-resistant Enterobacterales (CRE), understanding the interplay between iron uptake and antibiotic susceptibility could reveal new synergistic treatment strategies and resistance mechanisms.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating a quantitative measure of bacterial iron uptake into a standardized antimicrobial susceptibility testing (AST) platform will reveal distinct phenotypic profiles in CRE isolates, where impaired iron acquisition correlates with increased susceptibility to specific antibiotic classes.
proposals.method: We will adapt a high-throughput, fluorescence-based assay using the iron-responsive probe calcein-AM to measure real-time iron chelation and uptake in bacterial cells. This will be integrated into a modified Clinical and Laboratory Standards Institute (CLSI) broth microdilution format. For each clinical CRE isolate (collected from prior studies), a 96-well plate will contain serial antibiotic dilutions in iron-depleted and iron-replete media. Simultaneous optical density (growth) and fluorescence (iron uptake) readings will be taken using a plate reader. Data analysis will employ a custom algorithm to calculate minimum inhibitory concentrations (MICs) and derive a parallel 'Iron Uptake Inhibition Index' (IUII) for each antibiotic condition.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, standardized dual-function assay for clinical microbiology research. It will provide the first large-scale phenotypic data linking iron acquisition capacity to antibiotic susceptibility profiles in CRE. The findings could identify specific antibiotic-iron synergy pairs, informing future combination therapy trials and diagnostic development.
proposals.novelty: While iron-antibiotic interactions are known, no pre-2026 method seamlessly integrates a quantitative, real-time measure of a key virulence trait (iron uptake) with standardized AST in a single assay suitable for high-throughput clinical isolate screening.

proposals.title: A Syndromic, Phenotype-Driven Algorithm for Empiric Gram-Negative Bacteremia Therapy: A Retrospective Cohort Study Using Clinical and Rapid Diagnostic Metagenomic Data
proposals.motivation: Rapid diagnostic tests (RDTs) and even metagenomic sequencing often identify pathogens but not their resistance profiles, leaving clinicians to choose empiric therapy. For bloodstream infections, delays in effective therapy increase mortality. A strategy to select optimal empiric therapy based on patient phenotype and syndromic presentation, rather than awaiting precise organism identification, could improve outcomes and combat overuse of broadest-spectrum agents like carbapenems.
proposals.hypothesis: A treatment algorithm based on readily available clinical variables (e.g., infection source, recent healthcare exposure, local epidemiology) and rapid phenotypic markers (e.g., Gram stain morphology from positive blood cultures) can predict the probability of resistance to standard empiric regimens (e.g., ceftriaxone, piperacillin-tazobactam) with accuracy sufficient to guide initial therapy, independent of final microbial identification.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study of patients with Gram-negative bacteremia from 2018-2024 at a multi-hospital system. Using electronic health record data, we will extract candidate predictors: demographics, comorbidities, healthcare exposures, infection source, prior culture data, and local antibiograms. The primary outcome will be in vitro non-susceptibility to guideline-recommended empiric regimens. We will use multivariable logistic regression and machine learning (LASSO regression and random forest) to develop a predictive model. Model performance will be assessed via cross-validated area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, clinically actionable algorithm to guide empiric therapy for Gram-negative bacteremia before final identification and susceptibility results. It will directly address antimicrobial stewardship by potentially reducing unnecessary carbapenem use. The findings will challenge the paradigm that precise pathogen identification is a prerequisite for optimal initial therapy.
proposals.novelty: While predictive models for resistance exist, this proposal uniquely integrates rapid metagenomic data (e.g., from the researcher's prior work on long-read sequencing of rectal swabs) as a potential real-time predictor of resistance gene carriage in the gut microbiome, combining it with classic clinical variables to form a novel, syndromic decision-support tool.

proposals.title: Comparative Clinical Outcomes of Monotherapy vs. Combination Therapy for Severe Carbapenem-Resistant Enterobacterales Bloodstream Infections: A Multi-Center Retrospective Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: Despite the availability of newer, potent single agents like ceftazidime-avibactam and meropenem-vaborbactam for treating carbapenem-resistant Enterobacterales (CRE) infections, high mortality persists. Clinical evidence from pre-2026 studies is conflicting on whether combination therapy, even with an active single agent, offers a survival benefit over monotherapy for severe infections like bloodstream infections. This gap directly impacts antimicrobial stewardship and patient care decisions in hospitals.
proposals.hypothesis: For patients with severe CRE bloodstream infections, combination antimicrobial therapy (utilizing an active single agent plus a second in vitro non-susceptible agent) will be associated with lower 30-day all-cause mortality compared to active monotherapy, independent of underlying patient severity.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-center, retrospective cohort study of adult patients with CRE bloodstream infections from 2020-2025, identified via clinical microbiology records. The primary exposure will be definitive therapy categorized as active monotherapy (e.g., ceftazidime-avibactam alone) vs. combination therapy (e.g., ceftazidime-avibactam plus aztreonam, fosfomycin, or an aminoglycoside). We will use multivariable logistic regression and propensity score matching to adjust for confounders including Pitt bacteremia score, source control, and underlying resistance mechanisms (e.g., presence of metallo-β-lactamases) identified via molecular epidemiology data.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide robust, real-world evidence to guide therapy for severe CRE infections, directly informing antimicrobial stewardship programs and treatment guidelines. It will clarify the role of 'double-coverage' combination regimens in an era of novel β-lactam/β-lactamase inhibitors and help define patient subgroups most likely to benefit.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies have examined combination therapy, this proposal uniquely integrates detailed molecular characterization of resistance mechanisms (informed by Tamma's prior work) with rigorous clinical outcomes analysis to test the sufficiency of monotherapy, moving beyond in vitro synergy to patient-centered endpoints.

proposals.title: Predicting the Emergence of Carbapenem-Resistant Enterobacterales during Treatment Using Patient-Specific Ecological Competition Models
proposals.motivation: The rise of carbapenem-resistant Enterobacterales (CRE) is a critical public health threat, complicating treatment and increasing mortality. Current clinical practice and epidemiological models often fail to predict the rapid emergence of resistance within individual patients during antibiotic therapy, as they do not fully account for the complex ecological dynamics of the gut microbiome, including competition between resistant and susceptible strains. A predictive framework is urgently needed to guide more precise, patient-specific antibiotic selection to suppress resistance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating patient-specific gut microbiome composition data with mechanistic ecological competition models can accurately forecast the in vivo population dynamics of CRE and the emergence of resistance during antibiotic treatment.
proposals.method: We will develop a computational pipeline using patient rectal swab metagenomic sequencing data (pre-2026 long-read mNGS methods) to quantify the abundance of specific CRE strains and their susceptible competitors. These data will parameterize a modified Lotka-Volterra competition model, where antibiotic pressure is modeled as a time-dependent selective killing function. The model will be calibrated and validated using longitudinal clinical data from patients colonized with CRE who received antibiotic therapy (e.g., meropenem, newer agents from Merck/Shionogi), with outcomes of resistance amplification measured by subsequent culture and sequencing.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, translatable modeling framework that moves beyond population-level epidemiology to predict resistance dynamics at the patient level. It will provide a proof-of-concept for using microbiome data to simulate treatment outcomes, directly informing the 'antibiotic selection' decision point faced by clinicians and antimicrobial stewardship (PharmD) teams.
proposals.novelty: While ecological models are used in theoretical epidemiology, their direct application to forecast resistance emergence in individual patients using their real-time, strain-resolved metagenomic data represents a significant and non-obvious advance over traditional, static susceptibility testing and genotypic surveillance.

proposals.title: A Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA) of Empirical Antibiotic Protocols for Gram-Negative Bloodstream Infections in a High-ESBL Prevalence Setting
proposals.motivation: Antimicrobial resistance (AMR) in Gram-negative bacteria, particularly extended-spectrum beta-lactamase (ESBL)-producing Enterobacterales, is a critical pre-2026 public health threat. Empirical antibiotic treatment for serious infections like bloodstream infections (BSIs) is often protocol-driven, but protocol failures due to unrecognized resistance or delays in diagnostic results lead to poor patient outcomes and drive broader-spectrum antibiotic use. A systematic, proactive approach to identifying vulnerabilities in these protocols is urgently needed.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying the engineering risk-assessment tool Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA) to institutional empirical antibiotic protocols for Gram-negative BSIs will identify high-risk, modifiable failure modes (e.g., delays in susceptibility reporting, inadequate initial coverage for prevalent resistance mechanisms) that are associated with measurable clinical outcomes.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-disciplinary FMEA with a team of infectious diseases physicians, clinical pharmacists (PharmD), microbiologists, and hospital epidemiologists. The process will: 1) Map the detailed treatment pathway from blood culture draw to definitive therapy for Gram-negative BSIs at our institution. 2) For each step, the team will identify potential failure modes, their causes, and effects, scoring them for severity, occurrence, and detection using pre-2026 FMEA standards. 3) Risk Priority Numbers (RPNs) will be calculated to rank failures. 4) High-RPN failures will be validated against a retrospective cohort of patients with ceftriaxone-resistant Enterobacterales BSIs (using methods from our prior molecular epidemiology work) to quantify their real-world impact on time to effective therapy and clinical outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a validated, prioritized list of actionable vulnerabilities in empirical antibiotic protocols. It will provide a novel framework for antimicrobial stewardship programs to proactively improve treatment pathways, potentially reducing time to effective therapy and minimizing unnecessary broad-spectrum antibiotic exposure. The methodology can be adapted for other infection syndromes and resistance threats.
proposals.novelty: While FMEA is established in high-reliability industries and some hospital processes, its structured application to antimicrobial treatment protocols, integrated with validation via clinical and molecular epidemiology data on resistant infections, represents a novel cross-disciplinary approach to AMR stewardship.

proposals.title: A Randomized, Placebo-Controlled Trial of Oral Fosfomycin for Decolonization of Asymptomatic Carriers of Carbapenem-Resistant Enterobacterales (CRE) in High-Risk Patients
proposals.motivation: Asymptomatic colonization with multidrug-resistant gram-negative bacteria (MDR-GNB) like CRE is a major reservoir for subsequent infections, particularly in immunocompromised or ICU patients, driving morbidity and mortality. Current guidelines lack evidence-based recommendations for decolonization, leading to inconsistent practice and potential overuse of last-resort agents like polymyxins. A targeted, evidence-based decolonization strategy is urgently needed to reduce infection risk and preserve broader antimicrobials.
proposals.hypothesis: A short-course, oral regimen of fosfomycin will be safe and more effective than placebo in eradicating gastrointestinal colonization with CRE in high-risk patients, as measured by serial rectal surveillance cultures, without promoting significant resistance.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-center, double-blind, randomized controlled trial. High-risk patients (e.g., hematology-oncology, planned ICU admission) identified as CRE carriers via rectal swab surveillance will be randomized to receive oral fosfomycin (3g every 48h for 4 doses) or placebo. The primary outcome is microbiological clearance at day 14, assessed using both standard culture and long-read metagenomic sequencing of rectal swabs to characterize the gut microbiome and resistome dynamics. Secondary outcomes include incidence of subsequent CRE infection at 90 days, safety, and emergence of fosfomycin resistance genes via molecular epidemiology of isolates.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-level evidence from an RCT on the efficacy and safety of a specific oral agent for MDR-GNB decolonization. It will generate critical data on the ecological impact of decolonization on the gut resistome and microbiome, informing future stewardship. The results will directly guide clinical practice for infection prevention in high-risk populations.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has characterized colonization, this prospectively tests a pragmatic decolonization regimen. It uniquely integrates clinical trial design with deep molecular characterization (long-read metagenomics and β-lactamase epidemiology) to assess efficacy and unintended consequences simultaneously.

proposals.title: A Modeling Framework to Translate Individualized Microbiome-Resistome Data into Population-Level Infection Control Policy for Carbapenem-Resistant Enterobacterales
proposals.motivation: Antimicrobial resistance (AMR) is a critical public health threat, with carbapenem-resistant Enterobacterales (CRE) posing a particular risk. While advanced diagnostics like long-read metagenomic sequencing provide detailed patient-level colonization data, there is a significant translational gap in using this granular information to inform effective, scalable public health interventions. Current infection control policies often lack the precision to target high-risk reservoirs and transmission pathways efficiently.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating patient-level genomic and epidemiological data into an agent-based model will identify key, modifiable transmission nodes and allow for the simulation of targeted public health interventions, leading to more effective and resource-efficient population-level CRE control strategies.
proposals.method: We will develop an agent-based model (ABM) using a modified version of the EMOD framework. The model will be parameterized with real-world data from our prior studies, including long-read metagenomic sequencing results from CRE-colonized patients (rectal swabs) and molecular epidemiology of β-lactamases in bloodstream infections. Patient agents will be characterized by their unique resistome profiles and contact networks within a simulated healthcare system. We will test interventions such as targeted screening based on resistome signatures, contact isolation protocols, and antibiotic stewardship rules (e.g., restricting aminopenicillins for specific resistotypes), measuring outcomes on CRE prevalence and incidence.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, data-driven modeling framework that explicitly links individual microbiome-resistome diagnostics to population-level outcomes. It will provide actionable evidence for public health officials and hospital epidemiologists on which combination of interventions is most likely to curb CRE spread, moving beyond one-size-fits-all policies towards precision public health.
proposals.novelty: This approach is novel in its direct integration of high-resolution, patient-specific long-read metagenomic data (revealing complete resistome and plasmid contexts) into a dynamic transmission model to evaluate and optimize infection control policies, a synthesis not yet common in pre-2026 AMR research.

proposals.title: Inferring Probable β-Lactamase-Mediated Resistance Mechanisms from Clinical Response Patterns in Gram-Negative Bloodstream Infections: A Machine Learning Approach
proposals.motivation: Current diagnostics for antimicrobial resistance (AMR) rely on direct detection of pathogens or resistance genes, which can be delayed or unavailable, especially for complex resistance mechanisms like ESBLs and carbapenemases. This gap forces clinicians to rely on empiric therapy, potentially leading to poor outcomes. An inverse modeling approach that deduces likely resistance from readily available clinical data (e.g., treatment regimen, microbiological clearance, fever resolution) could provide a real-time, phenotype-informed decision support tool when molecular results are pending.
proposals.hypothesis: That supervised machine learning models can be trained on retrospective clinical outcome data to accurately predict the presence of specific β-lactamase resistance mechanisms (e.g., ESBL, KPC, OXA-48-like) in patients with Enterobacterales bloodstream infections, based on their initial treatment response and patient covariates.
proposals.method: We will build a retrospective cohort of patients with ceftriaxone-resistant or carbapenem-resistant Enterobacterales bacteremia, with documented treatment regimens and outcomes (e.g., 72-hour clinical response, time to blood culture clearance). Molecular epidemiology data (e.g., β-lactamase gene profiles from whole-genome sequencing) will serve as the ground truth. We will train and compare multiple classifiers, including gradient boosting machines (XGBoost) and regularized logistic regression, using features encompassing initial antibiotic therapy (drug, dose, timing), basic patient factors (comorbidities, source), and early laboratory trends. Model performance will be evaluated via cross-validation and on a held-out test set.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a proof-of-concept predictive model that translates clinical response into a probabilistic assessment of underlying resistance mechanisms. It will provide a novel, data-driven framework to support antimicrobial stewardship and guide early therapy escalation or de-escalation. The findings could inform the development of future clinical decision support systems embedded in electronic health records.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Tamma et al. has characterized the molecular epidemiology of resistance, this study inverts the paradigm by using treatment outcomes to infer mechanisms, rather than using mechanisms to predict outcomes. It applies machine learning to a novel clinical inference problem distinct from standard genotype-phenotype prediction tasks.

proposals.title: Evaluating Pharmacodynamic Dosing Strategies to Mitigate the Risk of AmpC Derepression During Third-Generation Cephalosporin Therapy for Enterobacterales Infections
proposals.motivation: Third-generation cephalosporins (3GCs) are widely avoided for serious infections caused by AmpC-producing Enterobacterales (e.g., Enterobacter cloacae complex, Citrobacter freundii, Serratia marcescens) due to the risk of inducible resistance and treatment failure. This leads to increased use of broader-spectrum agents like carbapenems, contributing to antimicrobial resistance. A pre-2026 knowledge gap exists regarding whether optimized, high-intensity 3GC dosing regimens can suppress AmpC derepression and yield clinical success, potentially preserving carbapenems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a pharmacodynamic-optimized, high-dose, extended-infusion regimen of a third-generation cephalosporin (e.g., ceftriaxone or cefotaxime), potentially in combination with a second agent, will achieve clinical and microbiological cure rates non-inferior to carbapenem therapy for bloodstream infections caused by AmpC-producing Enterobacterales with baseline susceptibility.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-center, retrospective cohort study of patients with AmpC-producer bloodstream infections from 2020-2025, identified via electronic health record review and confirmed by whole-genome sequencing for β-lactamase genes. Using pharmacokinetic/pharmacodynamic (PK/PD) modeling, we will simulate exposures for various 3GC dosing regimens (e.g., ceftriaxone 2g q12h, cefotaxime 2g q8h as extended infusion). The primary outcome will be a composite of 30-day clinical failure and emergence of resistance, compared between patients receiving PK/PD-optimized 3GC regimens and those receiving carbapenems, using propensity score-adjusted multivariate analysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide evidence-based, specific dosing recommendations for the potential safe use of 3GCs against AmpC producers, directly addressing a common clinical dilemma. It will contribute to antimicrobial stewardship by defining a pathway to reduce carbapenem consumption. The integration of genomic confirmation of AmpC with detailed PK/PD modeling will offer a robust methodological framework for future studies of optimized dosing against inducible resistance.
proposals.novelty: While avoidance of 3GCs is standard, this study proactively tests a high-dose suppression strategy rather than simply documenting failure. It moves beyond descriptive epidemiology to a therapeutic intervention model, combining advanced molecular diagnostics (long-read sequencing for precise resistome characterization) with sophisticated PK/PD simulations to challenge a core clinical axiom.

proposals.title: Development and Clinical Validation of an Aptamer-Based Lateral Flow Assay for the Rapid Detection of Plasmid-Mediated AmpC β-Lactamases in Enterobacterales
proposals.motivation: AmpC β-lactamases, particularly plasmid-mediated variants like CMY, DHA, and FOX, confer resistance to broad-spectrum cephalosporins and are often misidentified as extended-spectrum β-lactamases (ESBLs) by routine phenotypic tests. This misidentification leads to inappropriate carbapenem use, driving further resistance. Current molecular confirmation (e.g., PCR, whole-genome sequencing) is slow, costly, and unavailable at the point-of-care, creating a critical diagnostic gap in both hospital and community settings.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that synthetic nucleic acid aptamers, selected against conserved epitopes of prevalent plasmid-mediated AmpC variants, can be engineered into a stable, low-cost lateral flow assay (LFA) that provides rapid (<30 min), equipment-free visual detection directly from bacterial colonies or positive blood cultures.
proposals.method: We will employ Systematic Evolution of Ligands by Exponential Enrichment (SELEX) against purified CMY-2, DHA-1, and FOX-1 β-lactamases to generate high-affinity DNA aptamers. Optimal aptamers will be conjugated to gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) and immobilized on nitrocellulose strips in a sandwich LFA format, with a capture line of anti-aptamer antibodies. The assay will be validated using a characterized biobank of clinical Enterobacterales isolates (n=200) with known resistance genotypes from prior studies, benchmarking against PCR and phenotypic confirmatory tests (e.g., cefoxitin/cloxacillin disk synergy).
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first aptamer-based point-of-care test for AmpC detection, enabling rapid differentiation from ESBLs. It will provide a practical, low-complexity tool for clinical microbiology labs and resource-limited settings to guide targeted therapy (e.g., avoiding carbapenems for AmpC-hyperproducing E. cloacae), supporting antimicrobial stewardship efforts.
proposals.novelty: While aptamer-LFAs exist for toxins and viruses, their application for specific β-lactamase detection is novel. This approach moves beyond antibody-based LFAs by using chemically stable, modifiable aptamers that can target conserved enzyme features across variants, offering a potential advantage over variant-specific monoclonal antibodies.

proposals.title: Development and Clinical Validation of a Multiplex PCR and Lateral Flow Assay for the Rapid Phenotypic Confirmation of Plasmid-Mediated AmpC β-Lactamase Production in Enterobacterales
proposals.motivation: AmpC β-lactamase production, particularly plasmid-mediated (pAmpC), is a critical but often under-detected mechanism of resistance to third-generation cephalosporins in Enterobacterales. Current phenotypic detection methods (e.g., cefoxitin/cloxacillin disk tests, boronic acid synergy) are complex, slow, and lack specificity for distinguishing chromosomal from plasmid-mediated AmpC, leading to inappropriate antibiotic use and delayed effective therapy. A rapid, point-of-care compatible diagnostic is urgently needed to guide timely antibiotic stewardship, especially for bloodstream and urinary tract infections.
proposals.hypothesis: A multiplex PCR assay targeting major pAmpC gene families (e.g., CMY, DHA, ACC, FOX) coupled with a rapid lateral flow immunoassay for phenotypic confirmation can accurately and rapidly (<4 hours) detect and confirm clinically significant pAmpC production directly from positive blood culture bottles or bacterial colonies.
proposals.method: We will design a multiplex PCR panel for six major pAmpC gene families using pre-2026 sequence databases. The amplicon will be integrated with a lateral flow assay (LFA) using antibodies specific to a conserved AmpC epitope, capturing only enzymatically active protein produced from PCR-positive strains. The assay will be validated against a characterized collection of clinical isolates (n=200) from recent molecular epidemiology studies, using whole-genome sequencing (Illumina) and phenotypic reference methods (disk diffusion, Etest) as gold standards. Performance will be assessed for sensitivity, specificity, and time-to-result compared to standard methods.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, integrated genotypic-phenotypic diagnostic tool that significantly reduces the time to detect pAmpC from 24-48 hours to under 4 hours. It will directly inform clinical decision-making for antibiotics like cefepime and carbapenems, supporting antimicrobial stewardship efforts. The assay format is designed for potential deployment in clinical microbiology laboratories without requiring extensive molecular infrastructure.
proposals.novelty: While PCR for pAmpC genes exists, the novel integration with a rapid phenotypic LFA confirmation step addresses the critical gap of distinguishing silent carriage from expressed resistance, a key limitation of purely molecular tests. This combined approach is not commercially available and moves beyond research-grade sequencing (like the applicant's prior mNGS work) to a practical, bedside-compatible diagnostic.

proposals.title: Optimizing Beta-Lactam Dosing to Suppress AmpC-Derepressed Mutants: A Pharmacodynamic Model Adapted from Agricultural Resistance Management
proposals.motivation: The emergence of resistance via AmpC derepression in pathogens like Enterobacter cloacae complex during beta-lactam therapy is a major clinical challenge, leading to treatment failure and limiting therapeutic options. Current dosing strategies are optimized for wild-type kill but may inadvertently enrich for pre-existing resistant subpopulations. There is a critical need for dosing frameworks that explicitly manage the selection pressure for resistance, akin to strategies used in agriculture to delay pest resistance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating principles from agricultural pest resistance management into a pharmacodynamic model will identify beta-lactam dosing regimens (e.g., specific drug concentrations, infusion durations, or combination sequences) that effectively suppress the competitive expansion of AmpC-derepressed mutants while maintaining efficacy against the wild-type population.
proposals.method: We will develop a hybrid pharmacokinetic/pharmacodynamic (PK/PD) and population dynamics model. The model will simulate competition between wild-type and AmpC-derepressed subpopulations of Enterobacterales under time-varying antibiotic concentrations. Using in vitro PK/PD data (e.g., from hollow-fiber infection models) and published mutation rates, we will parameterize the model. We will then employ optimization algorithms (e.g., Monte Carlo simulations coupled with gradient descent) to test and rank dosing regimens of drugs like cefepime or piperacillin-tazobactam based on a fitness function that penalizes mutant expansion.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, quantitative framework for designing antibiotic dosing strategies that explicitly aim to delay resistance. It will generate testable, optimized regimen candidates for subsequent validation in preclinical models. The project will bridge ecological modeling from agriculture with clinical pharmacology, offering a new paradigm for antimicrobial stewardship.
proposals.novelty: While PK/PD models for resistance exist, the direct application of formal pest resistance management optimization strategies to bacterial populations within a host is novel. This approach systematically treats resistant mutants as a 'pest' population to be managed through strategic drug 'application' timing and intensity.

proposals.title: A Machine Learning Framework Integrating Genomic and Clinical Data to Predict AmpC Induction Risk and Guide Optimal Antibiotic Therapy in Enterobacterales Infections
proposals.motivation: AmpC β-lactamase induction in Enterobacterales poses a significant clinical challenge, leading to treatment failure with broad-spectrum cephalosporins and complicating antibiotic stewardship. Current methods for predicting induction risk are limited, often relying on phenotypic testing or single-gene detection, which fail to capture the complex interplay of genetic determinants and patient-specific factors. A predictive tool is urgently needed to guide optimal antibiotic selection, particularly for high-risk patients, to improve outcomes and curb resistance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a machine learning model trained on integrated long-read metagenomic sequencing data from rectal swabs (capturing plasmid contexts and promoter variants) and structured clinical data (e.g., prior antibiotic exposure, comorbidities) will accurately predict the risk of AmpC induction and subsequent treatment failure, outperforming models based on single data modalities.
proposals.method: We will develop a supervised learning framework using retrospective data from patients with Enterobacterales bloodstream and urinary tract infections. Features will include: 1) genomic features from long-read sequencing (e.g., ampC gene variants, promoter mutations, plasmid replicon types, co-resistance genes), and 2) clinical features (e.g., recent β-lactam exposure, infection site, severity scores). We will compare the performance of ensemble methods (Random Forest, XGBoost) and a feed-forward neural network. Models will be trained to predict a binary outcome of treatment failure or microbiological persistence linked to AmpC induction.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a validated, interpretable predictive model for a key resistance mechanism, directly informing antibiotic decision-making at the point of care. It will advance the field of genomic epidemiology by demonstrating the added value of integrating plasmid-level genomic data with clinical variables for resistance prediction, moving beyond simple gene detection.
proposals.novelty: While ML has been applied to resistance prediction, this proposal uniquely focuses on the dynamic, inducible AmpC mechanism and leverages the detailed genetic context provided by long-read metagenomic sequencing of surveillance samples, a method recently advanced by the researcher, to build a more robust predictor.

proposals.title: Establishing a Global, Real-Time Genomic Surveillance Network for Tracking the Dissemination of Plasmid-Mediated AmpC β-Lactamases in Enterobacterales
proposals.motivation: AmpC β-lactamases, particularly plasmid-mediated variants like CMY, DHA, and ACC, confer resistance to broad-spectrum cephalosporins and are increasingly reported worldwide, complicating treatment for serious infections. Current surveillance, reliant on phenotypic data and local molecular epidemiology, lacks the resolution and speed to trace the international transmission of these mobile resistance determinants, hindering effective containment strategies. A scalable, real-time genomic network is urgently needed to map the global flow of AmpC genes and their associated plasmids.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating long-read sequencing data from a distributed network of clinical and public health laboratories will reveal previously unrecognized, rapid intercontinental transmission routes of specific AmpC plasmid lineages and host strains, enabling predictive modeling of their spread.
proposals.method: We will establish a pilot network with 8-10 sites across North America, Europe, and Asia, leveraging existing clinical collaborations (e.g., with Merck, Shionogi). Participating sites will process rectal swabs or bloodstream isolates using standardized long-read metagenomic (Nanopore) or isolate sequencing protocols. A centralized bioinformatics pipeline, built on established tools (e.g., Flye for assembly, ABRicate for resistance gene detection, MOB-suite for plasmid typing), will perform real-time analysis. Data will be integrated into a cloud-based dashboard (using architectures like Nextstrain) for phylogenetic visualization and spatiotemporal tracking of AmpC gene variants and plasmid backbones.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first proof-of-concept, real-time genomic surveillance system specifically for plasmid-mediated AmpC resistance, moving beyond retrospective studies. It will generate an open-access, global dataset linking AmpC genotypes to plasmid epidemiology, providing public health agencies and pharmaceutical partners (e.g., for novel β-lactam/β-lactamase inhibitor development) with actionable intelligence on emerging threats.
proposals.novelty: While genomic networks exist for pathogens like influenza or SARS-CoV-2, and some for carbapenemases, a dedicated, real-time system for AmpC-producing Enterobacterales is novel. This proposal uniquely focuses on plasmid ecology and uses near-patient long-read sequencing from rectal swabs to capture colonization dynamics, not just clinical infections.

proposals.title: A Real-Time Genomic Surveillance and Modeling Framework for Guiding the Regional Deployment of AmpC-Activated Prodrugs
proposals.motivation: AmpC β-lactamases are a major cause of resistance to broad-spectrum cephalosporins in Enterobacterales, yet their diversity and variable expression complicate treatment. Current surveillance often reports only phenotypic resistance, lacking the variant-level genomic data needed for targeted prodrug design. This gap prevents a precision approach to deploying novel AmpC-activated prodrugs, which are in development by companies like Merck and Shionogi, risking suboptimal use and rapid resistance emergence.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that establishing a real-time genomic surveillance network to characterize circulating AmpC variants and their expression levels will enable the creation of predictive models to guide the rational design and region-specific deployment of tailored prodrugs, thereby improving therapeutic efficacy and delaying resistance.
proposals.method: We will establish a surveillance network across mid-Atlantic hospitals to collect clinical isolates from bloodstream and urinary tract infections. Using long-read metagenomic sequencing (Oxford Nanopore) on rectal swabs and isolates, we will characterize the resistome, identify specific AmpC alleles (e.g., CMY, DHA, ACT), and quantify expression via promoter/attenuator mutations. Data will be integrated into a cloud-based dashboard (Therapeutic Intelligence Network) using a graph database (Neo4j) to model variant epidemiology. Machine learning models (e.g., gradient boosting) will predict regional enzymatic activity profiles to inform prodrug candidate selection.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first real-time, variant-specific map of AmpC epidemiology, directly linking surveillance to therapeutic development. It will produce a scalable framework for precision antibiotic deployment and establish a feedback loop to monitor the impact of novel prodrugs on resistance evolution, offering a new model for adaptive stewardship.
proposals.novelty: While surveillance networks exist, none are explicitly designed to feed real-time, enzyme-specific data directly into the prodrug design and deployment pipeline, creating a closed-loop system for a precision antimicrobial strategy.

proposals.title: Development and Feasibility Assessment of a Primary Care Clinical Decision Support Platform for the Identification and Management of Intestinal AmpC β-lactamase-Producing Enterobacterales Carriage
proposals.motivation: Intestinal carriage of AmpC-producing Enterobacterales (AmpC-E) is a significant reservoir for community-onset and healthcare-associated infections, often leading to treatment failures with extended-spectrum cephalosporins. Current decolonization protocols are complex and confined to specialist infectious disease settings, leaving a critical gap in primary care where high-risk patients are first encountered. A scalable, point-of-care strategy is urgently needed to shift AmpC-E control from reactive hospital treatment to proactive community-based prevention.
proposals.hypothesis: A structured, algorithm-driven clinical decision-support platform, when implemented in primary care clinics, will enable general practitioners to accurately identify high-risk patients for AmpC-E carriage and safely administer targeted decolonization, thereby reducing the prevalence of intestinal carriage.
proposals.method: We will develop a web-based platform integrating a validated risk prediction model (incorporating factors like recent antibiotic use, healthcare exposure, and travel history) with step-by-step clinical workflows. The platform will guide users from risk assessment through selection of screening (e.g., rectal swab culture or PCR) to prescription of an evidence-based, gut-selective decolonization regimen (e.g., oral non-absorbable aminoglycosides or colistin). We will conduct a prospective, single-arm feasibility study across 5-10 primary care clinics, enrolling high-risk patients identified by the platform to assess protocol adherence, decolonization success (via post-treatment culture), and safety.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first validated tool to operationalize AmpC decolonization in primary care, directly addressing a key antimicrobial resistance (AMR) prevention gap. It will generate crucial pilot data on the feasibility and preliminary efficacy of community-driven decolonization, providing a model for translating complex ID stewardship into general practice.
proposals.novelty: While decision-support exists for hospital antibiotic prescribing, this platform uniquely targets the *preventive* management of a specific resistance mechanism (AmpC) in the *community* setting, bridging a critical translational gap between hospital-based stewardship and primary care practice.

proposals.title: Development and Validation of a Logic-Based Point-of-Care Tool for the Management of AmpC Beta-Lactamase-Producing Enterobacterales Infections in Non-Specialist Settings
proposals.motivation: AmpC beta-lactamase-producing organisms, such as Enterobacter cloacae complex and Citrobacter freundii, pose a significant clinical challenge. Inappropriate antibiotic use, particularly with third-generation cephalosporins, can induce resistance and lead to treatment failure. This risk is heightened in primary care and non-ID specialty settings where clinicians may lack the specialized knowledge to interpret complex antibiograms and resistance mechanisms.
proposals.hypothesis: A simplified, binary logic-flow diagnostic tool, analogous to an electrical circuit breaker troubleshooting guide, can safely and effectively guide non-specialist clinicians in the risk assessment and antibiotic selection for suspected AmpC-producing infections, improving adherence to evidence-based guidelines.
proposals.method: We will develop a decision-support algorithm based on a synthesis of pre-2026 IDSA guidelines and published clinical risk factors (e.g., prior 3GC exposure, infection source). The tool's architecture will be a deterministic, stepwise binary tree (IF-THEN-ELSE logic) implemented as a mobile application. Validation will occur via a two-phase study: first, a retrospective chart review comparing tool-recommended therapy to actual therapy in patients with confirmed AmpC producers; second, a prospective simulation study with general practitioners, hospitalists, and pharmacists using clinical vignettes to assess usability and accuracy against expert ID adjudication.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a validated, practical tool that translates complex antimicrobial stewardship principles into actionable frontline guidance. It aims to reduce inappropriate cephalosporin use, mitigate induction of resistance, and improve patient outcomes by empowering a broader range of clinicians. The project will also generate data on the knowledge gaps and decision-making patterns of non-specialists managing these pathogens.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its direct adaptation of engineering 'circuit breaker' fault-tree analysis logic to a clinical microbiology problem, moving beyond traditional educational interventions to an algorithmic, point-of-care solution designed explicitly for the non-specialist.

proposals.title: A High-Throughput Broth Disk Elution Platform Coupled with PK/PD Modeling to Predict and Suppress Caspofungin Resistance Evolution in Candida spp.
proposals.motivation: Antifungal resistance, particularly echinocandin resistance in Candida species, is a growing public health threat with limited treatment options. Current susceptibility testing and PK/PD models are not optimized to predict the dynamic emergence of resistant subpopulations under clinically relevant, fluctuating drug concentrations, hindering stewardship and personalized therapy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that time-kill data generated via a high-throughput broth disk elution method, when integrated into a mathematical PK/PD model adapted from oncology, can accurately simulate and identify antifungal dosing regimens that suppress the outgrowth of resistant fungal mutants in vitro.
proposals.method: We will adapt the broth disk elution method to create time-varying caspofungin concentrations against Candida albicans and C. glabrata isolates. High-throughput time-kill data will be generated using optical density and CFU counts. These data will parameterize a PK/PD model based on the Simeoni tumor growth inhibition framework, modified to include susceptible and resistant fungal subpopulations. The model will be used to simulate clinical PK profiles (e.g., from Merck's caspofungin label) to predict mutant suppression.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, scalable laboratory platform for simulating antifungal treatment scenarios. It will provide a proof-of-concept PK/PD modeling strategy to rationally design dosing regimens that minimize resistance selection, directly informing antimicrobial stewardship and clinical trial design.
proposals.novelty: The fusion of the simple, scalable broth disk elution technique with sophisticated, oncology-derived evolutionary PK/PD models to preemptively combat antifungal resistance is a novel, interdisciplinary approach not previously applied in medical mycology.

proposals.title: Development and Clinical Validation of an Integrated Point-of-Care Diagnostic and Decision Support System for Plasmid-Mediated AmpC β-Lactamase Detection in Urinary Tract Infections
proposals.motivation: Plasmid-mediated AmpC (pAmpC) β-lactamases in Enterobacterales, particularly in E. coli and K. pneumoniae, are a growing public health threat. They confer resistance to broad-spectrum cephalosporins and can be misidentified as extended-spectrum β-lactamases (ESBLs) by standard tests, leading to inappropriate carbapenem use and treatment failure. In primary care and emergency departments, where rapid diagnostics and specialist consultation are limited, this diagnostic ambiguity directly compromises patient outcomes and accelerates antimicrobial resistance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating a rapid, point-of-care molecular diagnostic for pAmpC genes with an automated, guideline-based clinical decision support algorithm will improve the accuracy and timeliness of appropriate antibiotic selection for suspected Gram-negative infections in front-line clinical settings.
proposals.method: We will develop a lateral flow assay using recombinase polymerase amplification (RPA) targeting a consensus sequence for prevalent pAmpC families (e.g., CMY, DHA). The assay result will be input into a rule-based clinical decision support algorithm, coded in a lightweight architecture (e.g., a local web app or integrated device software). The algorithm will process the pAmpC result alongside user-input patient data (allergy history, infection severity, local susceptibility patterns) to generate specific, actionable recommendations (e.g., avoid cefoxitin/3rd-gen cephalosporins, consider piperacillin-tazobactam or a carbapenem based on severity, order confirmatory testing). The integrated system will be validated using a biobank of rectal swabs and urine samples from patients with known CRE colonization or UTI.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a first-of-its-kind integrated diagnostic-therapeutic tool that closes the management gap for pAmpC infections in resource-limited settings. It will provide empirical data on the feasibility and potential impact of embedding interpretative guidance directly into a rapid diagnostic device, moving beyond mere detection to directed therapeutic action. The project will generate a validated prototype and a clinical workflow model for antimicrobial stewardship in primary care.
proposals.novelty: While point-of-care molecular diagnostics and standalone clinical decision support software exist, their fusion into a single, simplified workflow for complex resistance mechanisms like pAmpC is novel. This approach specifically addresses the interpretative challenge for non-specialists, translating a complex genotypic result into a context-specific therapeutic recommendation at the point of need.

proposals.title: Exploiting AmpC Hyperexpression: A Targeted Prodrug Strategy for Carbapenem-Resistant Enterobacterales
proposals.motivation: Carbapenem-resistant Enterobacterales (CRE), particularly AmpC-hyperproducing strains, represent a critical public health threat with limited therapeutic options. Current broad-spectrum antibiotics cause significant collateral damage to the host microbiome, contributing to resistance spread and adverse outcomes. A strategy that selectively targets resistant pathogens while sparing commensals is urgently needed to improve patient outcomes and preserve our antimicrobial arsenal.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a sub-inhibitory 'priming' dose of a beta-lactam can be used to selectively induce AmpC hyperexpression at an infection site, and that a novel prodrug, activated specifically by AmpC, will then deliver a potent cytotoxin to the targeted bacteria, turning their resistance mechanism into a lethal vulnerability.
proposals.method: We will first use clinical CRE isolates (e.g., Enterobacter cloacae complex) to characterize AmpC induction dynamics in vitro using sub-inhibitory cefoxitin via RT-qPCR and enzyme activity assays. Second, we will synthesize and screen a library of prodrug candidates based on the structure of membrane-disrupting antimicrobial peptides (e.g., polymyxin B nonapeptide derivatives) linked to AmpC-specific beta-lactamase substrates (e.g., cephalosporin cores). Third, we will evaluate the selective bactericidal activity of the lead prodrug candidate in combination with the priming agent using time-kill assays against isogenic AmpC-inducible and constitutive strains. Finally, cytotoxicity against human cell lines and activity against commensal gut flora will be assessed.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide proof-of-concept for a novel, pathogen-selective antibacterial strategy inspired by ADEPT. It will deliver a new chemical entity (the prodrug) and a defined two-step treatment protocol designed to minimize off-target effects. The findings could establish a new paradigm for targeting antibiotic-resistant bacteria by exploiting their resistance enzymes.
proposals.novelty: While beta-lactamase-activated prodrugs have been explored, the strategic use of a sub-therapeutic 'priming' agent to locally upregulate the activating enzyme specifically in resistant pathogens, thereby creating a therapeutic window, is a novel application of the ADEPT concept to antibacterial therapy.

proposals.title: Development and Clinical Validation of a Point-of-Care Disk Test with Integrated Decision Support for AmpC β-Lactamase Detection in Enterobacterales
proposals.motivation: AmpC β-lactamase production in Enterobacterales is a major driver of cephalosporin resistance, yet detection remains challenging outside reference labs. This leads to inappropriate antibiotic use, treatment failures, and poor stewardship in community and general hospital settings. A simplified, accessible diagnostic is urgently needed to guide effective therapy and close the gap between complex microbiology and frontline care.
proposals.hypothesis: A novel, simplified phenotypic disk test, when coupled with a rule-based clinical decision-support algorithm, can accurately detect AmpC production and generate actionable, guideline-concordant antibiotic recommendations comparable to expert interpretation.
proposals.method: We will develop a simplified test using a single cefoxitin disk combined with a boronic acid inhibitor on Mueller-Hinton agar, optimized for a short (6-8 hour) incubation. The test result (zone diameter difference) will be input into a locally hosted, intuitive web application. This application will employ a deterministic, rule-based algorithm (using CLSI and EUCAST breakpoints pre-2026) to interpret the phenotype, integrate local antibiogram data (e.g., for meropenem, cefepime, piperacillin-tazobactam), and output clear management guidance, including antibiotic options and suggested consultations.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated, low-complexity phenotypic test for AmpC detection accessible to non-specialist labs. It will also provide a prototype clinical decision-support tool that embeds specialist knowledge, aiming to improve antibiotic appropriateness for infections caused by AmpC-producing organisms in general practice.
proposals.novelty: The fusion of a purposefully simplified, rapid phenotypic test with an automated, interpretative decision-support system tailored for the generalist clinician represents a novel approach to democratizing complex antimicrobial resistance diagnostics and management.

proposals.title: Harmonizing Real-World Evidence for AmpC β-Lactamase Treatments: A Meta-Analysis Using Clinical/Bacteriological Disease Eradication as a Pragmatic Reference Standard
proposals.motivation: Treatment of infections caused by AmpC β-lactamase-producing Enterobacterales is challenging, with guidelines relying on low-certainty evidence. Observational studies report heterogeneous outcomes (e.g., clinical cure, microbiological clearance, mortality), precluding meaningful meta-analysis and leaving clinicians without clear comparative effectiveness data for antibiotics like cefepime, carbapenems, and piperacillin-tazobactam. A pragmatic method is needed to synthesize this noisy, real-world data.
proposals.hypothesis: Clinical/Bacteriological Disease Eradication (CBDE), a composite endpoint commonly reported in antibiotic trials, can serve as a pragmatic reference standard to harmonize heterogeneous observational data via bias-adjustment models, enabling a robust network meta-analysis of treatment effectiveness for AmpC-producing organisms.
proposals.method: We will systematically identify observational studies (2010-2025) reporting treatments for AmpC infections. Using a Bayesian network meta-regression framework, we will model study-reported outcomes (e.g., 30-day mortality, clinical failure) as functions of a latent, study-specific CBDE probability. Prior distributions for bias parameters will be informed by pre-2026 trial data where CBDE and other endpoints are co-reported. This will calibrate across study designs, allowing estimation of adjusted comparative effects (odds ratios) for key antibiotic comparators.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first adjusted, quantitative synthesis of real-world effectiveness for antibiotics against AmpC producers, directly informing therapeutic decision-making. It will also establish a methodological framework for harmonizing disparate observational endpoints in infectious diseases meta-analysis, moving beyond simple exclusion of heterogeneous studies.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the traditional evidence hierarchy by using a pragmatic, trial-derived endpoint (CBDE) to enable rigorous inference from observational data, rather than dismissing it. The application of bias-adjustment models from network meta-analysis to harmonize real-world outcomes for antimicrobial resistance is novel.

proposals.title: A Hierarchical Bayesian Meta-Analysis Model to Correct for Heterogeneous Laboratory Measurement Error in Studies of Antimicrobial Susceptibility and Clinical Outcomes
proposals.motivation: Meta-analyses of antimicrobial treatment outcomes, crucial for stewardship, are confounded by heterogeneity from inconsistent laboratory methods. Pre-2026 literature documents significant variability in critical susceptibility tests, such as colistin disk diffusion potency and carbapenemase detection assays, which introduces systematic bias into pooled efficacy estimates. This methodological noise obscures true clinical effect sizes, potentially leading to incorrect conclusions about optimal therapies for resistant pathogens like AmpC-producing Enterobacterales.
proposals.hypothesis: Explicitly modeling laboratory measurement error as a structured random effect within a meta-analytic framework will significantly reduce heterogeneity and bias in pooled estimates of antimicrobial treatment efficacy derived from observational studies.
proposals.method: We will develop a hierarchical Bayesian measurement error model. The model will treat reported study-level outcomes (e.g., mortality, clinical cure) as functions of both a true underlying treatment effect and a study-specific error term parameterized by known methodological variables (e.g., disk potency lot, breakpoint guidelines year, use of molecular vs. phenotypic AST). Prior distributions for error components will be informed by pre-2026 quality control studies (e.g., CLSI/EUCAST reproducibility data). We will fit the model using Hamiltonian Monte Carlo via Stan, applying it to a curated dataset of studies on treatments for AmpC-producing and carbapenem-resistant Enterobacterales infections.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated statistical framework for deconvolving clinical efficacy from laboratory artifact in evidence synthesis. It will yield more reliable, adjusted estimates of treatment effects for antimicrobial stewardship committees. The model will be implemented as open-source R/Stan code, enabling direct application by researchers and clinicians to future meta-analyses.
proposals.novelty: While meta-analyses commonly model clinical heterogeneity, explicitly parameterizing and correcting for technical measurement error as a source of variance is a novel extension. This approach moves beyond simple subgroup analysis to statistically adjust for the documented imprecision of microbiological diagnostics.

proposals.title: Multi-Center Validation of the Carbapenem Breakpoint Enhancement (CBDE) Test for Predicting Clinical Outcomes in Diverse Gram-Negative Bacilli
proposals.motivation: The Carbapenem Breakpoint Enhancement (CBDE) test is a promising phenotypic method for identifying carbapenemase-producing Enterobacterales (CPE) and predicting carbapenem susceptibility in isolates with elevated MICs. However, initial validation studies have been limited in scale and geographic scope, primarily focusing on specific collections. As carbapenem resistance continues to spread globally, a robust, widely applicable diagnostic tool is urgently needed to guide therapy and improve outcomes for infections caused by multi-drug resistant Gram-negative bacilli.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the CBDE test will demonstrate high sensitivity and specificity for predicting clinical carbapenem efficacy across a large, geographically diverse collection of Gram-negative bacilli with non-susceptible carbapenem MICs, and that its performance will be consistent regardless of underlying resistance mechanisms (e.g., different carbapenemase types, porin loss, efflux).
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-center, retrospective study utilizing archived clinical isolates of Enterobacterales and non-fermenters (e.g., Pseudomonas aeruginosa, Acinetobacter baumannii) collected from 2018-2025 across three U.S. regions (Mid-Atlantic, Northeast, Southwest). Isolates with meropenem or imipenem MICs of ≥2 µg/mL will be included (n=~500). The CBDE test will be performed as described, using meropenem and imipenem disks with and without a defined enhancer (e.g., aminophenylboronic acid and clavulanate). Results will be compared against a composite reference standard: whole-genome sequencing for resistance gene detection and clinical outcome data (where available) from electronic health records to assess treatment success with carbapenem-based regimens.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the most comprehensive pre-clinical validation of the CBDE test to date, establishing its generalizability across diverse bacterial species and resistance mechanisms. The findings will directly inform clinical microbiology laboratories and antimicrobial stewardship programs about the utility of this test for refining carbapenem breakpoints and optimizing therapy for challenging infections, potentially supporting future regulatory evaluation.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies have characterized the CBDE test, this will be the first to rigorously evaluate its performance against a large, geographically diverse collection that includes non-fermenters and correlates results with both molecular epidemiology and real-world clinical outcomes, moving beyond in vitro concordance.

proposals.title: Mechanistic Investigation of Carbapenem Breakpoint Discrepancies in mcr-1-Positive Escherichia coli: A Genomic and Phenotypic Analysis
proposals.motivation: The mobile colistin resistance gene mcr-1 poses a critical threat to last-line polymyxin therapy. Carbapenem Breakpoint Discrepancy Evaluation (CBDE) is a valuable tool for detecting carbapenemase production, but emerging reports suggest it may yield errors for mcr-1-positive isolates. Understanding this phenomenon is essential for accurate antimicrobial resistance (AMR) diagnostics and stewardship, as misclassification could impact treatment decisions for infections caused by these multidrug-resistant pathogens.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the presence of the mcr-1 gene or its associated plasmid alters membrane permeability or efflux activity in Escherichia coli, leading to subtle changes in carbapenem susceptibility profiles that are misinterpreted by the CBDE algorithm.
proposals.method: We will assemble a well-characterized collection of mcr-1-positive E. coli clinical isolates from our institution's biorepository and collaborating sites (e.g., Merck, Shionogi surveillance networks). For each isolate, we will perform long-read whole-genome sequencing (Oxford Nanopore) to obtain complete plasmid sequences and identify co-existing resistance determinants. Concurrently, we will conduct comprehensive phenotypic testing, including reference broth microdilution for carbapenems and colistin, and analyze the results using the standard CBDE algorithm. We will then correlate specific genomic features (plasmid incompatibility groups, efflux pump regulators, porin mutations) with CBDE error patterns using statistical modeling.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first detailed mechanistic explanation for CBDE performance issues with mcr-1-harboring strains. It will directly inform clinical microbiology laboratories and AMR surveillance programs about a potential diagnostic pitfall. Furthermore, findings may refine CBDE interpretation criteria or lead to updated testing guidelines for isolates with plasmid-mediated colistin resistance.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has characterized the molecular epidemiology of mcr-1, no study has systematically investigated its unexpected interference with carbapenemase detection algorithms like CBDE. This proposal uniquely bridges detailed genomic analysis with practical diagnostic microbiology to solve a specific, unresolved clinical laboratory problem.

proposals.title: A Microfluidic Single-Cell Platform for Standardized Profiling of Heterogeneous AmpC β-Lactamase Induction Dynamics in Clinical Enterobacterales Isolates
proposals.motivation: Inducible AmpC β-lactamase expression in pathogens like Enterobacter cloacae complex is a major cause of β-lactam treatment failure, as standard phenotypic tests (e.g., the cefoxitin disk-based induction test, CBDE) provide only a binary, population-averaged result. This fails to capture clinically significant heterogeneity in induction thresholds and kinetics within a bacterial population, which may explain unpredictable therapeutic outcomes. A standardized, high-resolution tool is needed to quantify this heterogeneity and its potential link to clinical risk.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that clinical isolates of AmpC-producing Enterobacterales harbor sub-populations with distinct, quantifiable AmpC induction profiles, and that this single-cell heterogeneity correlates with in vitro phenotypic resistance outcomes better than traditional CBDE results.
proposals.method: We will design a polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) microfluidic device featuring an array of microwells to trap individual bacterial cells from pre-characterized clinical isolates. The device will integrate programmable pneumatic valves to deliver precise gradients and pulses of cefoxitin inducer. Real-time, single-cell AmpC expression will be quantified using a fluorescent reporter (e.g., a transcriptional fusion of the ampC promoter to gfp). Time-lapse imaging and custom image analysis pipelines (using CellProfiler and custom Python scripts for fluorescence tracking and threshold detection) will generate induction curves for hundreds of individual cells per isolate.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first standardized, single-cell 'induction phenotype' for AmpC-producing bacteria, moving diagnostics beyond a binary call. It will establish a novel metric—the sub-population induction structure—that can be tested for association with treatment failure risk in future clinical studies. The microfluidic platform itself represents a new tool for antimicrobial susceptibility testing at single-cell resolution.
proposals.novelty: While microfluidics have been used for single-cell antibiotic susceptibility testing, their application to standardize and deconstruct the heterogeneous dynamics of a specific, inducible resistance mechanism (AmpC) is novel. This approach transforms a static, phenotypic test into a dynamic, quantitative single-cell assay.

proposals.title: Rapid Phenotypic Antimicrobial Susceptibility Testing Directly from Positive Blood Cultures Using a Modified Carbapenemase Detection Enhancement (CBDE) Method
proposals.motivation: Bloodstream infections caused by resistant Enterobacterales and other pathogens require urgent, targeted therapy. Current workflows involve subculturing positive blood cultures to isolate colonies for AST, delaying results by 24-48 hours. A method for direct, rapid phenotypic AST from positive bottles would significantly shorten time to effective antibiotic therapy, potentially improving patient outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: The Carbapenemase Detection Enhancement (CBDE) method, which uses a fluorescent β-lactamase substrate to detect hydrolysis, can be adapted to work directly with broth from positive blood culture bottles to provide reliable, same-day phenotypic antimicrobial susceptibility results for key β-lactam antibiotics.
proposals.method: We will adapt the CBDE protocol for direct testing from BacT/ALERT or BACTEC positive blood culture bottles. Following Gram stain confirmation, samples will be processed using a standardized centrifugation and washing protocol to reduce inhibitors. The CBDE assay will be performed using a panel of β-lactam antibiotics (e.g., ceftriaxone, cefepime, meropenem) combined with the fluorescent substrate. Fluorescence kinetics will be measured using a plate reader, and results will be interpreted using algorithm-defined breakpoints for hydrolysis rates, validated against standard CLSI broth microdilution results from isolated colonies.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated protocol for performing direct phenotypic AST from positive blood cultures, generating results within a single work shift. It will contribute a practical tool for antimicrobial stewardship programs and clinical microbiology laboratories to accelerate de-escalation or escalation of therapy. The study will also characterize the performance of this adapted CBDE method against a diverse collection of bloodstream pathogens, including those producing ESBLs and carbapenemases.
proposals.novelty: While CBDE has been used for carbapenemase detection from colonies, its adaptation for comprehensive, direct-from-blood-culture-bottle AST for a panel of β-lactams is novel. This approach bypasses the subculture step entirely, offering a phenotypic alternative to molecular methods that detect only known resistance genes.

proposals.title: A Clinical-Microbiomic Model to Predict Treatment Outcomes for Carbapenem-Resistant Enterobacterales Infections Independent of In Vitro Susceptibility
proposals.motivation: In vitro susceptibility testing (AST) for multidrug-resistant organisms (MDROs) like carbapenem-resistant Enterobacterales (CRE) has significant limitations, including delayed results, phenotypic-genotypic discordance, and poor prediction of clinical outcomes in complex infections. This gap forces clinicians to rely on imperfect data, potentially leading to treatment failure or unnecessary toxicity. A strategy that integrates patient-specific factors with rapid molecular data could provide more reliable, real-time therapeutic guidance.
proposals.hypothesis: A predictive model integrating patient clinical metadata, host immune markers, and real-time metagenomic resistome profiling from rectal swabs or infected sites will more accurately predict clinical success or failure of a targeted antibiotic regimen for CRE infections than standard AST results alone.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective cohort study of hospitalized patients with CRE colonization or infection. For each, we will collect clinical data (comorbidities, source, prior antibiotics), host inflammatory markers (e.g., procalcitonin), and perform long-read metagenomic sequencing (Oxford Nanopore) on rectal or wound swabs to characterize the resistome and microbiome. Clinical outcomes (treatment success/failure) will be the primary endpoint. We will train a multivariable logistic regression model, followed by a random forest classifier, to identify the combination of features most predictive of outcome, intentionally excluding traditional AST results as a primary variable.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a proof-of-concept framework for 'AST-agnostic' treatment decision support, directly addressing a critical clinical dilemma. It will identify specific, actionable clinical and microbiomic predictors of success for CRE infections, moving the field toward more personalized, real-time management strategies that complement traditional microbiology.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has characterized the resistome or epidemiology of CRE, this proposal uniquely seeks to build a predictive clinical tool that deliberately bypasses the limitations of conventional AST by synthesizing host, pathogen, and ecological data into an integrated outcome model.

proposals.title: A Multi-Criteria Decision Analysis Framework for Cost-Effective Empiric Antibiotic Selection in Gram-Negative Bloodstream Infections
proposals.motivation: Antimicrobial stewardship programs face the dual challenge of optimizing patient outcomes and managing escalating drug costs, particularly for newer agents like ceftazidime-avibactam. Existing treatment guidelines prioritize clinical efficacy, often neglecting cost and accessibility, which can strain hospital budgets and limit equitable care. A formal framework to balance these competing priorities is urgently needed.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a multi-criteria decision analysis (MCDA) model, integrating local antimicrobial resistance patterns, drug acquisition costs, and patient-specific risk factors, can identify empiric antibiotic regimens that are non-inferior in clinical efficacy but significantly more cost-effective than current standard-of-care protocols.
proposals.method: We will develop a discrete-event simulation model using data from our prior molecular epidemiology studies on ceftriaxone-resistant Enterobacterales. The model will incorporate local susceptibility data, wholesale acquisition costs (e.g., for meropenem-vaborbactam, ceftolozane-tazobactam), and patient comorbidity scores. A weighted linear model algorithm will be employed within the MCDA framework, assigning and iteratively optimizing weights for efficacy (based on expected susceptibility), cost, and institutional formulary constraints through sensitivity analysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a transparent, data-driven tool for stewardship committees to justify formulary decisions and create institution-specific treatment algorithms. It will generate publishable evidence on the potential cost savings and ecological impact of strategically prioritizing older, narrower-spectrum agents where susceptibility is likely.
proposals.novelty: While cost-effectiveness analyses exist, this approach is novel in its real-time integration of local genomic resistance data (from methods like long-read metagenomics) into a pragmatic decision-support algorithm, moving beyond theoretical modeling to actionable, institution-level guidance.

proposals.title: Computational Design of Phage Cocktails to Preemptively Counteract Predicted Carbapenem-Resistant Enterobacterales Escape Mutations
proposals.motivation: The rise of carbapenem-resistant Enterobacterales (CRE) represents a critical public health threat, with limited therapeutic options. Phage therapy is a promising alternative, but its efficacy is often undermined by the rapid evolution of bacterial resistance to phages during treatment. Current approaches are reactive, deploying phages after resistance emerges. A proactive strategy to design phages against predicted escape mechanisms could significantly improve therapeutic durability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in silico prediction of CRE resistance mutations to first-line phages, followed by the rational design or selection of secondary phages targeting those predicted escape variants, will yield a robust, pre-emptive phage cocktail that suppresses resistance evolution in vitro.
proposals.method: We will isolate CRE strains from our existing rectal swab biorepository and sequence them using long-read mNGS. Using established phage-host interaction models (e.g., PhageBoost, machine learning on protein-protein interaction data), we will predict high-probability bacterial escape mutations (e.g., in phage receptor genes like ompC, fhuA, or LPS biosynthesis genes) for a panel of candidate therapeutic phages. We will then computationally screen phage libraries (e.g., from the SEA-PHAGES database) or engineer phage tail fibers via in silico protein design (using Rosetta or AlphaFold2 for structure prediction) to bind the mutated receptors. The efficacy of the designed cocktail will be validated in time-kill and serial passage assays against the target CRE strains.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel computational-experimental framework for designing next-generation phage therapeutics. It will generate a proof-of-concept, pre-emptive phage cocktail with demonstrated resilience against resistance evolution for CRE. The methodology will be a translatable blueprint for extending this approach to other high-priority antimicrobial-resistant pathogens.
proposals.novelty: While phage cocktail design is established, the novelty lies in framing it as an 'inverse problem'—starting from predicted bacterial evolutionary responses to design phages, rather than simply combining phages with known current efficacy. This integrates pre-2026 predictive genomics and protein design tools specifically to outmaneuver anticipated resistance before it clinically emerges.

proposals.title: A Multicenter, Retrospective Cohort Study Evaluating Clinical Outcomes of Early Oral Step-Down Therapy for Gram-Negative Bloodstream Infections in Immunocompromised Hosts
proposals.motivation: Immunocompromised patients with serious infections are traditionally treated with prolonged intravenous (IV) antibiotics due to concerns about oral bioavailability and infection relapse. This practice increases hospital length of stay, healthcare costs, and risks of IV line complications. In the pre-2026 era, while oral step-down is gaining traction for select infections in immunocompetent hosts, robust safety data in high-risk immunocompromised populations (e.g., solid organ transplant, hematologic malignancy) are critically lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: In immunocompromised patients with Gram-negative bloodstream infections who achieve initial clinical stability, early oral step-down therapy with highly bioavailable agents (e.g., fluoroquinolones, trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole, or novel β-lactam/β-lactamase inhibitor combinations) is non-inferior to continued IV therapy for the composite endpoint of 30-day all-cause mortality or recurrent infection.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multicenter, retrospective cohort study at three academic medical centers. The cohort will include immunocompromised adults (2018-2025) with monomicrobial Enterobacterales or non-fermenter bloodstream infections.
