Using propensity score matching based on severity, pathogen, and immune deficit, we will compare patients transitioned to oral therapy within ≤7 days of blood culture clearance to those receiving >7 days of IV therapy. The primary analysis will employ a Cox proportional hazards model to assess non-inferiority for the composite endpoint. Secondary outcomes include hospital length of stay, Clostridioides difficile infection, and acquisition of multidrug-resistant organisms, analyzed via multivariable regression.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first large-scale, comparative effectiveness data on oral step-down in a high-risk, heterogeneous immunocompromised population. It will inform evidence-based antimicrobial stewardship guidelines specific to this vulnerable group, potentially reducing unnecessary IV therapy and its associated burdens. The findings will also identify clinical and microbiological predictors of oral step-down success or failure.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies have examined oral step-down, they largely excluded or had minimal representation of profoundly immunocompromised hosts. This work directly addresses this evidence gap by focusing on this population and integrating detailed molecular epidemiology (e.g., β-lactamase genotypes from prior research) to assess how resistance mechanisms impact oral regimen selection and outcomes.

proposals.title: Comprehensive Genomic Characterization of Cefiderocol-Resistant Carbapenem-Resistant Enterobacterales: Identifying Mutations in Porins and Efflux Pumps Beyond NDM
proposals.motivation: Cefiderocol is a critical last-resort siderophore cephalosporin for treating carbapenem-resistant Enterobacterales (CRE), particularly those producing metallo-β-lactamases like NDM. However, emerging cefiderocol resistance threatens this therapeutic option. Pre-2026, resistance surveillance has primarily focused on acquired β-lactamase genes, leaving a knowledge gap regarding the contribution of chromosomal mutations in porins (e.g., OmpC, OmpF, OmpK35/36) and efflux pump regulators (e.g., CirA, TonB) to cefiderocol failure in clinical isolates.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that cefiderocol-resistant CRE isolates, including those with and without NDM, harbor distinct, recurrent mutations in genes encoding iron transport porins and efflux pump components, and that these mutations contribute significantly to the resistant phenotype independently of known carbapenemase genes.
proposals.method: We will perform long-read whole-genome sequencing (PacBio or Oxford Nanopore) on a retrospective collection of cefiderocol-resistant CRE clinical isolates (MIC ≥4 µg/mL) from our mid-Atlantic health system, previously characterized for β-lactamases. Using a custom bioinformatics pipeline, sequences will be assembled and annotated with Prokka. We will systematically screen for non-synonymous mutations and insertions/deletions in a predefined set of porin (ompC, ompF, ompK35, ompK36) and efflux/iron transport-related (cirA, tonB, exbB/D) genes by aligning to reference genomes (E. coli K-12, K. pneumoniae MGH 78578). Mutations will be correlated with phenotypic susceptibility profiles and compared to a control set of cefiderocol-susceptible isolates.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a detailed map of chromosomal mutations associated with cefiderocol resistance in clinical CRE, moving beyond a sole focus on acquired resistance genes. It will identify high-risk genotypes that could be monitored using genomic surveillance and inform mechanistic studies on cefiderocol uptake. The findings will directly aid clinical microbiologists and infectious diseases pharmacists in interpreting resistance reports and guiding therapy.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has described porin mutations in carbapenem resistance, a systematic, long-read sequencing-based search for these and efflux pump alterations specifically in clinical cefiderocol-resistant isolates, contextualized with precise β-lactamase data, represents a novel and targeted approach to understanding this emerging resistance threat.

proposals.title: Longitudinal Genomic and Phenotypic Analysis of Cefiderocol Resistance Emergence in NDM-Producing Enterobacterales Under Clinical Drug Pressure
proposals.motivation: Cefiderocol is a critical last-resort siderophore cephalosporin for treating carbapenem-resistant infections, particularly those caused by metallo-β-lactamase (MBL) producers like NDM. Pre-2026 surveillance indicates baseline resistance is rare, but isolated case reports suggest it can emerge rapidly during therapy. Understanding the genomic mechanisms and clinical risk factors for this resistance emergence is essential to preserve the drug's long-term efficacy and guide stewardship.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that cefiderocol resistance in NDM-producing Enterobacterales emerges rapidly under therapeutic drug pressure through specific, selectable genetic mutations or alterations in gene expression, and that these resistant mutants are associated with specific clinical and microbiological risk factors.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective, matched case-control study of patients with NDM-producing Enterobacterales infections treated with cefiderocol. Cases (emergence of resistance during therapy) will be identified via antimicrobial stewardship and clinical microbiology records. We will perform long-read metagenomic sequencing (Oxford Nanopore) on serial rectal swabs or clinical isolates from cases and matched controls (no resistance) to characterize the resistome and microbiome dynamics. Genomic analysis will focus on mutations in siderophore receptors (e.g., CirA, Fiu), porins, and efflux regulators. Clinical data (source control, pharmacokinetics, concomitant antibiotics) will be analyzed using multivariate logistic regression to identify risk factors.
proposals.contribution: This work will define the primary genetic pathways leading to cefiderocol resistance in NDM producers, a key knowledge gap. It will provide clinicians and stewardship programs with evidence-based risk factors to predict and potentially mitigate resistance emergence, informing treatment guidelines for these highly resistant infections.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies describe baseline resistance rates, this proposal uniquely applies longitudinal, high-resolution metagenomics directly to the clinical scenario of resistance emergence under drug pressure, linking genomic mechanisms with detailed patient-level treatment data.

proposals.title: A Phase 2b Clinical Trial Assessing the Clinical Efficacy and Safety of Ceftazidime-Avibactam Plus Aztreonam for the Treatment of Cefiderocol-Resistant, NDM-Producing Enterobacterales Infections
proposals.motivation: The global spread of New Delhi metallo-β-lactamase (NDM)-producing carbapenem-resistant Enterobacterales (CRE) represents a critical public health threat. Cefiderocol, a novel siderophore cephalosporin, has been a valuable last-resort agent, but resistance, particularly in NDM-harboring strains, is increasingly reported, leaving few viable treatment options. The in vitro synergy of ceftazidime-avibactam (which inhibits serine-β-lactamases) with aztreonam (which is stable to metallo-β-lactamases) is a promising theoretical strategy, but robust clinical efficacy data from prospective trials is urgently needed.
proposals.hypothesis: The combination of ceftazidime-avibactam and aztreonam will demonstrate superior clinical efficacy compared to historical or best available therapy in patients with serious infections caused by cefiderocol-resistant, NDM-producing Enterobacterales.
proposals.method: We propose a multi-center, open-label, single-arm Phase 2b clinical trial. Adult patients with documented bloodstream infections, complicated urinary tract infections, or hospital-acquired pneumonia due to cefiderocol-resistant (MIC ≥4 µg/mL), NDM-positive Enterobacterales will be enrolled. Participants will receive ceftazidime-avibactam (2.5 g q8h) plus aztreonam (2 g q8h) for 7-14 days. The primary endpoint will be clinical cure at Test of Cure (7 days post-therapy). Secondary endpoints include microbiological eradication, 30-day all-cause mortality, and safety. Isolates will undergo whole-genome sequencing and long-read metagenomic sequencing of rectal swabs to characterize resistance mechanisms and microbiome shifts.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first prospective clinical evidence for the efficacy of this specific dual-beta-lactam combination against a highly resistant pathogen subset. It will generate critical data to inform treatment guidelines and antimicrobial stewardship for NDM-CRE infections with limited options. The integrated genomic and microbiome analysis will elucidate mechanisms of treatment success or failure.
proposals.novelty: While in vitro synergy data exists, no prospective clinical trial has specifically targeted cefiderocol-resistant NDM producers to validate this combination's real-world utility. The study design directly addresses a critical gap identified from recent molecular epidemiology and microbiome surveillance work.

proposals.title: Quantifying the blaNDM-5 Copy Number Threshold Required for Cefiderocol Resistance in Carbapenem-Resistant Enterobacterales
proposals.motivation: Cefiderocol is a critical last-resort siderophore cephalosporin for carbapenem-resistant Enterobacterales (CRE), but resistance is emerging, often associated with blaNDM-5. Pre-2026, it is known that blaNDM copy number variation (CNV) on plasmids can elevate MICs, but the precise quantitative threshold at which CNV surpasses the CLSI/EUCAST clinical breakpoint for cefiderocol (S ≤2 µg/mL, R >2 µg/mL) is undefined. Defining this threshold is essential for predicting resistance from genomic data and understanding treatment failure risk in patients colonized or infected with CRE.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a specific, quantifiable increase in blaNDM-5 copy number, measured via long-read sequencing, is necessary and sufficient to elevate the cefiderocol minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) above the clinical resistance breakpoint in isogenic Escherichia coli and Klebsiella pneumoniae backgrounds.
proposals.method: We will construct isogenic strains in E. coli and K. pneumoniae backgrounds with incremental, defined increases in blaNDM-5 copy number using plasmid engineering and homologous recombination. Cefiderocol MICs will be determined in triplicate via broth microdilution per CLSI guidelines. blaNDM-5 copy number will be quantified absolutely using long-read Oxford Nanopore sequencing (MinION) with the Q20+ chemistry for high accuracy, aligning reads to reference plasmids with minimap2 and calculating copy number via read-depth analysis with tools like CopyNumber or custom bioinformatics pipelines. Statistical correlation between copy number and MIC will define the resistance threshold.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first experimentally defined copy number threshold for cefiderocol resistance mediated by blaNDM-5, a key carbapenemase. It will directly inform the clinical interpretation of long-read metagenomic and isolate sequencing data from surveillance studies, allowing for more accurate prediction of cefiderocol susceptibility. The findings will guide antimicrobial stewardship and the development of rapid molecular diagnostics for detecting high-risk resistance genotypes.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies correlate blaNDM with elevated MICs, this proposal uniquely aims to establish a precise, quantitative breakpoint for copy number-driven resistance to a specific last-line agent, cefiderocol, using controlled isogenic models and high-accuracy long-read sequencing, moving beyond observational associations.

proposals.title: Genomic Mechanisms of Resistance to Siderophore-Antibiotic Conjugates: Investigating Copy Number Amplification as a Common Pathway in Carbapenem-Resistant Enterobacterales
proposals.motivation: Siderophore-antibiotic conjugates, such as cefiderocol, represent a critical advance against multidrug-resistant Gram-negative pathogens. However, resistance mechanisms, including copy number amplification of siderophore receptor genes, threaten their efficacy. Understanding if this resistance pathway is a generalizable threat to other conjugates in development is essential for predicting clinical failure and designing next-generation agents.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that copy number amplification of iron-regulated outer membrane receptor genes is a common, inducible resistance mechanism to multiple siderophore-antibiotic conjugates beyond cefiderocol in carbapenem-resistant Enterobacterales (CRE).
proposals.method: We will employ long-read metagenomic next-generation sequencing (Oxford Nanopore) on rectal swabs and clinical isolates from a cohort of patients with CRE colonization/infection, building on our established pipeline. Using bioinformatic tools (Flye for assembly, minimap2 for alignment, and custom Python/R scripts), we will quantify baseline and induced copy number variation of siderophore receptor genes (e.g., cirA, fiu, iroN) in isolates exposed to sub-inhibitory concentrations of cefiderocol and experimental conjugates (e.g., those utilizing enterobactin or dicitrate scaffolds). Resistance phenotypes will be correlated with genomic amplifications via clinical microbiology assays.
proposals.contribution: This work will define the prevalence and genetic context of a high-impact resistance mechanism, providing crucial data for antimicrobial stewardship and clinical trial design for novel conjugates. It will generate a validated genomic surveillance method for detecting this resistance mechanism directly from patient samples, enhancing infection control.
proposals.novelty: While copy number amplification is recognized for cefiderocol, its role in resistance to other siderophore conjugates is unexplored. This study uniquely applies clinical metagenomics and molecular epidemiology to proactively assess resistance to an entire class of investigational agents, moving beyond single-drug analysis.

proposals.title: Clinical Outcomes of Treating Enterobacterales Bloodstream Infections with Agents Against Isolates Exhibiting Borderline Susceptibility or Heteroresistance: A Retrospective Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: Clinical susceptibility testing (e.g., broth microdilution, disk diffusion) provides categorical results (S/I/R) based on fixed breakpoints, but isolates near these breakpoints or with heteroresistant subpopulations may be misclassified. This is a critical knowledge gap for last-line agents like carbapenems and newer β-lactam/β-lactamase inhibitors. Treating such infections with guideline-recommended agents based on standard susceptibility reports may lead to unexpected treatment failure, prolonged bacteremia, or resistance emergence.
proposals.hypothesis: Patients with Enterobacterales bloodstream infections caused by isolates exhibiting borderline susceptibility or heteroresistance to the administered antimicrobial agent, despite being reported as susceptible, will experience significantly worse clinical outcomes (e.g., 30-day mortality, microbiologic failure) compared to patients infected with fully susceptible isolates.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study at two tertiary care hospitals (2020-2025). Using stored clinical isolates from monomicrobial Enterobacterales bloodstream infections, we will perform reference broth microdilution testing to identify isolates with minimum inhibitory concentrations (MICs) at the susceptibility breakpoint (e.g., meropenem MIC = 2 µg/mL) or one dilution below. We will screen these borderline isolates for heteroresistance using population analysis profiling (PAP). Clinical data will be extracted from electronic health records. Multivariable logistic regression will model the association between borderline/heteroresistant status and the composite primary outcome of 30-day mortality or persistent bacteremia >7 days, adjusting for confounders like source control and severity of illness.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first quantitative evidence of the clinical risk posed by borderline susceptible and heteroresistant Enterobacterales isolates in a real-world setting. It will inform clinical microbiology laboratories on the potential value of reporting MICs and investigating heteroresistance for critical pathogens. The findings could guide updates to antimicrobial stewardship protocols and influence future breakpoint setting by organizations like CLSI.
proposals.novelty: While heteroresistance is a known laboratory phenomenon, its clinical impact for Enterobacterales treated with guideline-recommended agents is poorly characterized. This study directly links precise MIC data and heteroresistance testing with detailed patient outcomes, moving beyond in vitro studies to assess real-world treatment failure risk.

proposals.title: Evaluating the Efficacy and Microbiome Impact of Oral Non-Absorbable Antibiotic Regimens for Decolonization of Carbapenem-Resistant Enterobacterales in High-Risk Patients
proposals.motivation: Carbapenem-resistant Enterobacterales (CRE) colonization is a major risk factor for subsequent invasive infection and mortality, particularly in immunocompromised and ICU patients. Current clinical practice is reactive, focusing on treating established infections with last-resort antibiotics, which drives further resistance. A proactive, decolonization-focused strategy could prevent infections, reduce transmission, and preserve critical antibiotics, aligning with antimicrobial stewardship goals.
proposals.hypothesis: A targeted, oral non-absorbable antibiotic decolonization regimen (e.g., gentamicin/colistin) will significantly reduce the intestinal burden of CRE and associated resistance genes without causing long-term detrimental shifts in the gut microbiome resistome or diversity in colonized patients.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective, multi-center pilot interventional study in patients identified as CRE carriers via rectal swab surveillance. Participants will receive a defined oral non-absorbable antibiotic regimen. We will employ long-read metagenomic sequencing (Oxford Nanopore or PacBio) on serial rectal swabs pre-, post-, and 30-days post-intervention to quantitatively assess changes in CRE abundance, plasmid-borne resistance genes, and overall microbiome taxonomic and resistome composition. Clinical outcomes (incident CRE infection) will be tracked for 90 days.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide one of the first high-resolution genomic assessments of a decolonization strategy's direct impact on the gut resistome. It will generate preliminary efficacy and safety data to inform larger randomized controlled trials and offer a concrete, stewardship-aligned protocol that could be adopted by antimicrobial stewardship programs (ASPs) and infection prevention teams.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has characterized colonization, this study actively intervenes and uses advanced long-read metagenomics to dissect the intervention's success and ecological cost, moving beyond culture-based decolonization studies to a systems-level understanding.

proposals.title: Adaptive Dosing of Novel β-Lactam/β-Lactamase Inhibitors for Carbapenem-Resistant Enterobacterales Infections: A Pharmacokinetic/Pharmacodynamic Modeling Study
proposals.motivation: The rise of carbapenem-resistant Enterobacterales (CRE) necessitates the use of novel β-lactam/β-lactamase inhibitor (BL/BLI) combinations like ceftazidime-avibactam and meropenem-vaborbactam. However, optimal dosing in critically ill patients is challenging due to altered pharmacokinetics (PK) and the narrow therapeutic window required to achieve pharmacodynamic (PD) targets and suppress resistance. Current fixed-dose regimens, derived from pre-licensure trials, may lead to suboptimal exposure, treatment failure, and further resistance development.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying adaptive dosing algorithms and therapeutic drug monitoring (TDM) strategies, informed by PK/PD models from precision oncology, will more reliably achieve target drug exposures for novel BL/BLIs in CRE infections compared to standard fixed dosing, thereby improving clinical outcomes and mitigating resistance.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective, observational PK study in patients receiving novel BL/BLIs for CRE infections (e.g., bloodstream, UTI). Serial plasma samples will be collected to define population PK models using non-linear mixed-effects modeling (NONMEM). These models will be linked to in vitro PD data and patient-specific minimum inhibitory concentrations (MICs) from long-read sequencing to simulate probability of target attainment. We will adapt a Bayesian forecasting algorithm, analogous to those used for methotrexate in oncology, to develop a web-based TDM tool that recommends real-time, adaptive dose adjustments based on sparse drug levels and clinical covariates.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first clinically validated, adaptive dosing framework for novel BL/BLIs in CRE infections, directly addressing a critical gap in antimicrobial stewardship. It will generate actionable data for clinicians and pharmacists to personalize therapy, potentially improving cure rates and prolonging the utility of these last-line agents. The project will also establish a methodological pipeline for applying precision medicine principles from oncology to infectious diseases.
proposals.novelty: While TDM for vancomycin and aminoglycosides is routine, its application to novel BL/BLIs is nascent and non-adaptive. This proposal innovatively transposes the sophisticated, model-informed precision dosing paradigms common in cancer chemotherapy (e.g., for 5-fluorouracil) to the management of multidrug-resistant bacterial infections, creating a dynamic feedback loop between pathogen genetics (resistome) and host PK.

proposals.title: In Silico Clinical Trial Simulation for Rapid Prototyping of Novel Antibiotic Combination Regimens Against Carbapenem-Resistant Enterobacterales
proposals.motivation: The rise of carbapenem-resistant Enterobacterales (CRE) represents a critical public health threat, with limited and often suboptimal treatment options. Traditional clinical trials for new antibiotic combinations are prohibitively slow, expensive, and ethically challenging in this critically ill population. A rapid, iterative method to evaluate potential regimens is urgently needed to guide clinical practice and future trial design.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a pharmacodynamic/ pharmacokinetic (PK/PD) simulation framework, calibrated with real-world clinical and microbiological data, can accurately predict the efficacy of novel antibiotic combinations against CRE and identify optimal dosing strategies faster than traditional methods.
proposals.method: We will develop a mechanistic, agent-based in silico clinical trial platform. The model will integrate population PK parameters from sources like Merck and Shionogi drug labels, PD indices (e.g., fT>MIC), and bacterial growth/kill dynamics. It will be populated with virtual patients derived from our cohort's electronic health records and characterized using genomic data (e.g., β-lactamase profiles from our molecular epidemiology work). We will simulate trials of candidate combinations (e.g., novel β-lactam/β-lactamase inhibitor pairs + adjunctive agents) against a virtual CRE population, iterating on dosing, duration, and patient subpopulations to optimize predicted clinical cure and suppression of resistance.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, rapid-prototyping tool for antimicrobial stewardship and pharmaceutical R&D, enabling the pre-screening of combination strategies for clinical utility. It will generate specific, testable hypotheses for optimal CRE treatment regimens based on pathogen resistance mechanisms, directly informing the design of future pragmatic clinical trials.
proposals.novelty: While PK/PD modeling exists, applying an iterative 'engineering design cycle'—simulate, analyze, redesign—specifically to antibiotic combination therapy for CRE is novel. This approach tightly integrates real-world genomic epidemiology and clinical data into a dynamic simulation to prototype regimens, moving beyond static susceptibility testing.

proposals.title: Development and Validation of a Clinical Decision Rule for De-escalation from Carbapenems in Patients with Carbapenem-Resistant Enterobacterales (CRE) Bloodstream Infections
proposals.motivation: Broad-spectrum antibiotic overuse, particularly carbapenems, drives antimicrobial resistance. While rapid diagnostics like long-read metagenomic sequencing can identify resistant pathogens, there is a critical lack of evidence-based, patient-specific criteria to guide safe de-escalation to narrower, targeted therapy, leading to prolonged broad-spectrum use. This gap contributes to adverse drug events, *Clostridioides difficile* infection, and further resistance selection.
proposals.hypothesis: A clinical decision rule incorporating early clinical response parameters, host factors, and detailed molecular resistance profiles (e.g., specific β-lactamase genes) can identify patients with CRE bacteremia for whom de-escalation to targeted, non-carbapenem therapy is non-inferior to continued carbapenem-based regimens in terms of clinical cure and mortality.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multicenter, retrospective cohort study of adult patients with CRE bloodstream infections (2018-2024) identified via the MIDAS network. Using electronic health record data, we will extract candidate variables: time to clinical stability, source control, comorbidities, and detailed molecular resistance data from prior studies (e.g., β-lactamase type from whole-genome sequencing). A multivariable logistic regression model will identify predictors of success in patients who were de-escalated. These predictors will be formalized into a decision rule and validated using bootstrap techniques.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first validated, data-driven clinical decision rule to guide antibiotic de-escalation in CRE infections, moving beyond general principles to actionable criteria. It will directly inform antimicrobial stewardship protocols and provide a framework for prospective interventional trials, aiming to reduce carbapenem exposure without compromising patient outcomes.
proposals.novelty: Existing literature defines de-escalation conceptually or reports its frequency; this study uniquely integrates detailed molecular epidemiology (e.g., distinguishing between KPC and OXA-48-like producers) with granular clinical data to create a predictive tool for a high-risk scenario where de-escalation is currently avoided due to safety concerns.

proposals.title: Clinical Outcomes of Carbapenemase-Producing Enterobacterales Infections Treated with Non-Beta-Lactam Combination Regimens: A Retrospective Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: Infections caused by carbapenemase-producing organisms (CPOs) represent a critical public health threat, often necessitating treatment with newer, expensive, and potentially toxic agents like ceftazidime-avibactam. This paradigm assumes older, non-beta-lactam antibiotics are ineffective, potentially overlooking viable, cost-effective therapeutic options. A systematic evaluation of clinical outcomes for CPO infections treated with older antibiotic combinations is urgently needed to expand the therapeutic arsenal and combat antimicrobial resistance.
proposals.hypothesis: For a significant subset of CPO infections, particularly those involving specific carbapenemase types (e.g., KPC) and infection sites (e.g., urinary tract), combination regimens of older, non-beta-lactam antibiotics (e.g., aminoglycosides, tetracyclines, nitrofurantoin, fosfomycin) achieve clinical cure rates comparable to those of newer beta-lactam/beta-lactamase inhibitor combinations.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-center, retrospective cohort study of patients with CPO infections (bloodstream, urinary tract, intra-abdominal) from 2020-2025. Using clinical microbiology data, we will identify isolates with confirmed carbapenemase production via PCR (e.g., for blaKPC, blaNDM, blaOXA-48). Patient cohorts will be defined by initial definitive therapy: 1) newer beta-lactam agents (e.g., ceftazidime-avibactam, meropenem-vaborbactam) and 2) non-beta-lactam combinations (e.g., tigecycline + aminoglycoside, nitrofurantoin + fosfomycin for UTIs). The primary outcome will be clinical cure at 30 days. We will use multivariable logistic regression, adjusting for severity (APACHE II), source of infection, and specific carbapenemase gene, to compare outcomes between groups.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide robust, real-world evidence on the efficacy of older antibiotic combinations against CPOs, directly informing antimicrobial stewardship and treatment guidelines. It will identify specific pathogen (carbapenemase type) and infection characteristics where these regimens are most viable, offering a framework for de-escalation strategies to preserve newer agents.
proposals.novelty: This work challenges the prevailing assumption that CPO infections uniformly require novel beta-lactams, systematically investigating a pragmatic, stewardship-focused treatment paradigm. It integrates molecular epidemiology (carbapenemase genotype) with detailed clinical outcome data to define treatable phenotypes, moving beyond in vitro susceptibility to real-world effectiveness.

proposals.title: Optimization of Phenotypic Assays and Breakpoint Analysis for Reliable Detection of Carbapenemase-Producing Enterobacterales with Borderline Carbapenem MICs
proposals.motivation: Carbapenemase-producing organisms (CPOs) with low-level or borderline carbapenem MICs (e.g., meropenem MICs of 1-2 µg/mL) are a critical diagnostic and infection control challenge. Pre-2026, standard phenotypic methods like the modified carbapenem inactivation method (mCIM) and automated susceptibility testing can yield false-negative or indeterminate results for these isolates, leading to missed detection and potential transmission. This gap is clinically significant as it delays appropriate infection prevention measures and can compromise patient outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a systematic optimization of incubation conditions and reagent concentrations in existing phenotypic carbapenemase detection assays, combined with a defined algorithm for interpreting borderline MICs, will significantly improve the sensitivity and specificity for detecting CPOs with low-level resistance.
proposals.method: We will assemble a well-characterized collection of Enterobacterales isolates (n≈200) with defined carbapenemase genes (e.g., KPC, OXA-48-like, NDM) and meropenem MICs of 0.5-4 µg/mL, alongside susceptible and high-level resistant controls. We will systematically modify key parameters of the mCIM/eCIM and carbapenemase Nordmann/Poirel (CNP) test, including carbapenem disk potency, incubation time, and bacterial inoculum density. Performance will be evaluated against a gold standard of whole-genome sequencing and long-read metagenomic sequencing (as used in Tamma's prior work) for carbapenemase gene detection. A decision-support algorithm will be developed using classification and regression tree (CART) analysis of the optimized phenotypic results and MIC data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide clinical microbiology laboratories with a validated, optimized protocol for phenotypic CPO detection that is robust for low-MIC isolates, reducing reliance on more expensive and slower molecular tests for screening. It will also deliver a practical interpretive algorithm for borderline MICs, directly informing infection prevention practices and antimicrobial stewardship decisions.
proposals.novelty: While existing methods are validated for clear-cut resistance, this study focuses specifically on the problematic 'borderline' zone, applying a systematic, iterative optimization of phenotypic assays rather than solely developing a new test. The integration of a data-driven clinical decision algorithm for MIC interpretation in this context is also novel.

proposals.title: Development and Clinical Validation of a Multiplex CRISPR-Cas12a Assay for the Rapid Detection of Major Carbapenemase Genes in Enterobacterales Isolates
proposals.motivation: The rapid spread of carbapenem-resistant Enterobacterales (CRE), driven by mobile carbapenemase genes like blaKPC, blaNDM, and blaOXA-48, poses a critical public health threat. Current molecular diagnostics, while accurate, often require centralized laboratory infrastructure, leading to delays in targeted therapy and infection control. A rapid, point-of-care compatible diagnostic tool is urgently needed to guide clinical and public health interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a multiplexed, isothermal CRISPR-Cas12a-based assay can be developed to specifically and sensitively detect the most prevalent carbapenemase genes directly from bacterial isolates, providing results in under 60 minutes without complex instrumentation.
proposals.method: We will adapt the SHERLOCK (Specific High-sensitivity Enzymatic Reporter unLOCKing) platform. Specific crRNA guides will be designed for conserved regions of blaKPC, blaNDM, blaOXA-48, and blaVIM. The assay will use recombinase polymerase amplification (RPA) for isothermal target pre-amplification, followed by Cas12a collateral cleavage of a fluorescent reporter. We will validate the assay against a well-characterized biobank of CRE isolates (including K. pneumoniae, E. coli) from our prior epidemiology studies, using long-read metagenomic sequencing as the gold standard comparator. Specificity will be tested against isolates harboring other β-lactamases (e.g., ESBLs).
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated, rapid prototype diagnostic assay for key carbapenemase genes. It will provide proof-of-concept for applying CRISPR diagnostics to antimicrobial resistance surveillance in clinical microbiology, potentially enabling faster therapeutic decisions and more precise infection control measures in hospital and resource-limited settings.
proposals.novelty: While CRISPR diagnostics have been applied to viral pathogens, their adaptation for multiplexed detection of bacterial carbapenemase genes from cultured isolates, integrated with a streamlined workflow suitable for near-patient testing, represents a novel application in clinical bacteriology.

proposals.title: Beyond Clinical Trial Endpoints: A Real-World Assessment of Cefiderocol Efficacy in Polymicrobial and Biofilm-Associated Carbapenem-Resistant Gram-Negative Infections
proposals.motivation: Cefiderocol, a siderophore cephalosporin, demonstrated non-inferiority in registration trials (CREDIBLE-CR, APEKS-NP) using traditional endpoints like clinical cure and microbiological eradication. However, real-world infections are often polymicrobial or involve biofilms, factors not fully captured in controlled trial settings. Given the critical need for effective agents against carbapenem-resistant organisms, it is essential to validate if trial success translates to these more complex clinical scenarios.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that traditional clinical trial endpoints inadequately predict cefiderocol treatment success in real-world, complex infections characterized by polymicrobial involvement or biofilm formation, leading to discrepancies between trial-reported and observed clinical efficacy.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multicenter, retrospective cohort study of patients treated with cefiderocol for carbapenem-resistant Gram-negative infections. The primary analysis will compare clinical success (a composite of survival, symptom resolution, and lack of recurrence) against the presence of polymicrobial infection (determined by culture or clinical metagenomic sequencing) and indirect markers of biofilm (e.g., device-associated infection). We will employ multivariable logistic regression to identify predictors of failure, adjusting for key confounders like source control and comorbidities. A sub-analysis will utilize long-read metagenomic sequencing (consistent with Tamma's prior work) on available specimens to characterize the resistome and microbial community structure in treatment failures.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide crucial real-world evidence on cefiderocol's effectiveness in complex infections, informing antimicrobial stewardship and treatment guidelines. It will generate hypotheses about microbial ecological factors influencing treatment outcomes and highlight potential limitations of traditional regulatory endpoints for novel antibiotics.
proposals.novelty: While real-world data on cefiderocol is emerging, this study uniquely integrates detailed microbiological characterization (including metagenomics) with clinical outcomes to directly test the sufficiency of trial endpoints for polymicrobial/biofilm contexts, a gap not addressed in the pivotal trials.

proposals.title: Population Pharmacokinetic Modeling and Monte Carlo Simulation to Define Cefiderocol Dosing Regimens for Carbapenem-Resistant Enterobacterales Infections in Critically Ill Patients
proposals.motivation: Cefiderocol is a critical last-line siderophore cephalosporin for carbapenem-resistant Enterobacterales (CRE) infections. However, its pharmacokinetic (PK) variability, especially in critically ill patients with altered renal function, augmented renal clearance, or specific infection sites like pneumonia or bloodstream infections, poses a significant challenge for achieving reliable pharmacodynamic (PD) targets. Defining evidence-based, optimized dosing regimens is urgently needed to maximize efficacy and prevent the emergence of resistance in this vulnerable population.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a population PK model informed by patient-specific clinical data and pathogen MIC distributions will identify that standard cefiderocol dosing fails to achieve optimal PD targets (e.g., 100% fT>MIC) for a substantial proportion of critically ill patients with CRE infections, and that novel, adaptive dosing strategies will be required.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective, observational PK study in critically ill patients receiving cefiderocol for CRE infections. Serial plasma samples will be collected to measure drug concentrations. Data on infection site, renal function (eGFR, CRRT), and albumin will be collected. A population PK model will be developed using non-linear mixed-effects modeling (NONMEM). The final model will be used in Monte Carlo simulations (10,000 subjects) to calculate the probability of target attainment (PTA) for various dosing regimens against contemporary CRE MIC distributions from our institution's surveillance.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive, patient population-specific PK/PD analysis for cefiderocol in a U.S. critical care setting. It will generate clinically actionable dosing recommendations, including potential for extended or continuous infusions and renal adjustment algorithms, directly applicable to stewardship programs. The data will inform future clinical trial design and support antimicrobial stewardship efforts for this vital agent.
proposals.novelty: While general cefiderocol PK is described, this study uniquely integrates real-world, patient-level PK data from a diverse critically ill cohort with local molecular epidemiology (including carbapenemase types from prior work) and uses advanced simulation to define infection site-specific dosing, moving beyond one-size-fits-all regimens.

proposals.title: In Vitro and Genomic Assessment of Cefiderocol Activity Against Multidrug-Resistant Burkholderia spp. and Achromobacter spp. Clinical Isolates
proposals.motivation: Cefiderocol is a novel siderophore cephalosporin with potent activity against common nonfermenters like Pseudomonas aeruginosa and Acinetobacter baumannii. However, its clinical utility against other problematic glucose-nonfermenting Gram-negative rods, such as Burkholderia cepacia complex (BCC) and Achromobacter species, remains inadequately characterized. These organisms cause severe infections in cystic fibrosis and immunocompromised patients and often harbor extensive intrinsic and acquired resistance, creating a critical unmet therapeutic need.
proposals.hypothesis: Cefiderocol will demonstrate potent in vitro activity against a contemporary collection of multidrug-resistant Burkholderia and Achromobacter clinical isolates, and this activity will correlate with specific genomic profiles, including the absence of known metallo-β-lactamase genes and the presence of siderophore receptor alleles.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective, laboratory-based study using a biobank of clinically obtained, non-duplicate BCC and Achromobacter spp. isolates (n=~100) from 2020-2025. Minimum inhibitory concentrations (MICs) for cefiderocol and comparators (e.g., ceftazidime-avibactam, trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole) will be determined via reference broth microdilution per CLSI guidelines. All isolates will undergo whole-genome sequencing using Illumina short-read platforms. Genomic analysis will involve species identification (MLST, cgMLST), resistome profiling (using curated databases like CARD and ResFinder), and correlation of MICs with the presence/absence of β-lactamase genes (particularly MBLs), mutations in siderophore receptors, and other putative resistance determinants.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide one of the first comprehensive, genotype-phenotype datasets on cefiderocol susceptibility for these challenging pathogens, directly informing therapeutic decisions for resistant infections. It will expand the evidence base for cefiderocol's spectrum and identify potential genomic markers of susceptibility or resistance, guiding future clinical trial design and diagnostic development.
proposals.novelty: While cefiderocol's activity against common nonfermenters is established, systematic evaluation against BCC and Achromobacter is sparse. This work uniquely integrates standardized phenotypic testing with detailed genomic analysis to elucidate mechanisms and predictors of activity in these less common but high-consequence species.

proposals.title: Engineering Isogenic Strains to Decipher the Genetic Determinants of Cefiderocol Resistance in Carbapenem-Resistant Enterobacterales
proposals.motivation: Cefiderocol is a critical last-resort siderophore cephalosporin for treating carbapenem-resistant Enterobacterales (CRE) infections. However, resistance is emerging, and its precise genetic mechanisms—involving complex interactions between β-lactamases, porin mutations, and siderophore receptors—remain poorly understood. This knowledge gap, highlighted by recent clinical epidemiology studies, hinders the development of diagnostics and stewardship strategies to preserve this vital antibiotic.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific, defined mutations in genes encoding β-lactamases (e.g., NDM, KPC variants), iron transporters (e.g., CirA, Fiu), and outer membrane porins (e.g., OmpC, OmpF) are necessary and sufficient to confer clinically relevant cefiderocol resistance in an isogenic background.
proposals.method: We will employ CRISPR-Cas9-based genome editing in a well-characterized, susceptible Escherichia coli or Klebsiella pneumoniae background to construct a panel of isogenic strains. Mutations will be introduced singly and in combinations, based on alleles identified in our prior clinical surveillance (e.g., NDM variants, OmpK35/36 truncations). Cefiderocol MICs will be determined via broth microdilution per CLSI guidelines (pre-2026). The contribution of each mutation will be quantified using growth kinetics under iron-limited conditions and competitive fitness assays.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a definitive, causal map of genetic variants driving cefiderocol resistance, moving beyond correlative clinical observations. It will establish a validated toolkit of engineered strains for future mechanistic studies of antibiotic permeation and for evaluating novel β-lactamase inhibitor combinations. The findings will directly inform the interpretation of clinical genomic surveillance data.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has described resistance patterns in clinical isolates, this proposal uses precise synthetic biology to deconvolute the individual and synergistic effects of resistance determinants in a controlled genetic background, a approach not yet widely applied to cefiderocol. This moves from epidemiological association to direct genetic proof.

ideas.title: Integrated Point-of-Care AmpC Detection and Automated Clinical Decision Support for Primary Care
ideas.description: This research integrates isothermal amplification or CRISPR-based point-of-care diagnostics for plasmid-mediated AmpC genes with an embedded, automated decision-support platform. The diagnostic device would not only provide a rapid 'yes/no' result for the resistance gene but would also feed this result, along with basic patient data input by the clinician, into a built-in or linked simplified algorithm. This system translates the complex implications of an AmpC-positive result into clear, actionable recommendations for antibiotic selection and next steps, tailored for the primary care or emergency department setting. The synergy transforms a complex microbiological finding into an immediate, guided therapeutic action, bridging the diagnostic-to-treatment gap for non-specialists and potentially improving outcomes.

ideas.title: A Simplified, Point-of-Care AmpC Detection and Management System for General Practice
ideas.description: This research fuses the concept of simplifying laboratory methodology with the goal of democratizing specialist knowledge. It aims to develop and validate a novel, streamlined phenotypic test for AmpC detection—such as a single-disk or short-gradient method—that is rapid and robust enough for use in community or general hospital labs. The results from this simplified test are then automatically interpreted by an integrated, intuitive clinical decision-support algorithm. This system provides clear, actionable guidance on antibiotic selection and patient management, effectively embedding specialist-level AmpC knowledge into a tool accessible to general practitioners and non-specialist clinicians, closing the gap between complex microbiology and frontline care.

ideas.title: Therapeutic Intelligence Network: Real-time surveillance to guide and deploy AmpC-activated prodrugs
ideas.description: This project integrates the development of AmpC-activated prodrugs with a global, real-time genomic surveillance network for AmpC-producing pathogens. The surveillance network will identify not just the presence of resistance but will characterize the specific AmpC variants and their expression levels in circulating strains. This data will then guide the rational design and regional formulation of prodrugs tailored to the prevalent enzymatic profiles, enabling precision deployment. Furthermore, the network will monitor the therapeutic impact and any subsequent evolution of resistance to the prodrugs, creating a dynamic feedback loop for adaptive antibiotic stewardship.

ideas.title: Community-Driven Decolonization: A Scalable, Point-of-Care Platform for AmpC Carriage Eradication in Primary Care
ideas.description: This research develops and validates an integrated clinical decision-support platform for primary care that guides general practitioners through identifying high-risk patients for intestinal AmpC carriage, selecting appropriate screening, and administering evidence-based decolonization regimens. The platform translates complex infectious disease protocols into actionable, step-by-step workflows, making prevention feasible outside specialist settings. By enabling front-line clinicians to safely manage carriage eradication, the project aims to shift AmpC control from reactive infection treatment in hospitals to proactive, community-wide prevention. This synergy could significantly reduce the reservoir of resistant bacteria and prevent downstream infections.

ideas.title: Adapting 'Circuit Breaker' Diagnostic Logic for Point-of-Care AmpC Management
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that managing infections involving AmpC beta-lactamase-producing organisms requires specialist knowledge. By drawing an analogy from electrical engineering, where complex circuit fault diagnosis was made accessible to field technicians via simplified troubleshooting trees and logic-based diagnostic tools, we can adapt this solution to infectious disease. The research would develop and validate a point-of-care clinical decision-support tool. This tool, likely a mobile application or structured paper protocol, would use a binary, stepwise logic flow (e.g., 'Does the patient have a history of third-generation cephalosporin exposure? Yes/No') to guide general practitioners through risk assessment, interpretation of local antibiogram data, and safe antibiotic selection, thereby making critical AmpC management knowledge actionable at the frontline of care without requiring deep infectious disease specialization.

ideas.title: Quantifying and correcting for measurement error heterogeneity in meta-analyses of antimicrobial susceptibility and treatment outcomes
ideas.description: This research proposes a novel meta-analytic approach that explicitly models laboratory measurement error, such as colistin disk potency variability, as a structured source of heterogeneity within observational studies on antimicrobial treatment (e.g., for AmpC producers). By treating this measurement uncertainty as a quantifiable random effect, the model can statistically adjust pooled estimates of treatment efficacy, correcting for bias introduced by diagnostic test variability. This allows for more reliable inference from inherently noisy real-world data, where clinical heterogeneity is confounded by methodological inconsistency. The outcome is a validated statistical framework that improves evidence synthesis for antimicrobial stewardship by deconvolving true clinical effect from systematic laboratory error.

ideas.title: Single-Cell CBDE: Microfluidic Standardization for Heterogeneous AmpC Induction Profiling
ideas.description: This research direction develops a microfluidic device that integrates a miniaturized and automated version of the standardized CBDE protocol. The device will trap individual bacterial cells in micro-wells or channels, expose them to defined gradients or pulses of beta-lactam inducers (like cefoxitin), and quantify AmpC expression dynamics in real-time using fluorescent reporters or other single-cell readouts. This approach will map the heterogeneity of induction thresholds and response times across a clinical isolate population. The resulting 'single-cell CBDE profile' provides a new diagnostic metric, moving beyond a simple positive/negative result to quantify the sub-population structure of inducible resistance, which could better predict treatment failure risk.

ideas.title: Aptamer-based lateral flow assay for point-of-care detection of AmpC β-lactamases
ideas.description: This research idea adapts a solution from the field of environmental toxin detection, where aptamer-based lateral flow assays have been developed for rapid, accurate, and accessible on-site testing of small molecules. The analogous solution uses synthetic nucleic acid aptamers as stable, cost-effective recognition elements in a simple paper-strip format, providing a visual result without specialized equipment. Adapted to the clinical microbiology context, the idea is to develop and validate a similar diagnostic test for detecting AmpC-mediated resistance. This would involve selecting specific aptamers against key AmpC β-lactamase variants, conjugating them to reporters like gold nanoparticles, and engineering a lateral flow assay that can rapidly distinguish AmpC-producing pathogens directly from clinical samples or bacterial colonies, thereby improving accessibility and speed of detection in routine laboratories and point-of-care settings.

ideas.title: Apply pest resistance management modeling from agriculture to suppress AmpC mutant selection
ideas.description: This idea applies the solution from agricultural pest resistance management to the problem of suppressing AmpC mutant selection during beta-lactam therapy. In agriculture, mathematical models of pest population dynamics are used to optimize pesticide application strategies (e.g., dose, timing, rotation) to suppress the emergence of resistant pest strains while maintaining crop protection. This analogous solution is adapted by modeling the bacterial population within a patient as a competing pest population. Specifically, pharmacokinetic/pharmacodynamic (PK/PD) models are integrated with models of bacterial population dynamics to simulate the competition between wild-type and AmpC-derepressed mutant bacteria under different beta-lactam dosing regimens. The goal is to identify optimized dosing strategies (concentration, frequency, duration) that maximize suppression of the wild-type pathogen while minimizing the selective pressure and competitive advantage conferred to pre-existing or emerging resistant mutants, thereby extending the clinical utility of these antibiotics.

ideas.description: This research fuses the concepts by using the broth disk elution method to create a simple, scalable system for generating time-kill data for antifungals like caspofungin against fungal populations. This high-throughput data is then fed into pharmacokinetic/pharmacodynamic (PK/PD) models, adapted from oncology, which are designed to predict the evolution of drug-resistant mutants. The synergy enables the rapid, laboratory-based simulation of clinical dosing scenarios to proactively identify antifungal regimens that maximize efficacy while suppressing the selection of resistant subpopulations, directly informing stewardship and personalized therapy to combat antifungal resistance.

ideas.title: Enzyme-Triggered Cytotoxin Prodrugs via Antibody-Directed Enzyme Prodrug Therapy (ADEPT) Analogy
ideas.description: This idea expands the original prodrug concept by drawing an analogy from the field of Oncology and Targeted Cancer Therapy, specifically Antibody-Directed Enzyme Prodrug Therapy (ADEPT). The analogous solution involves a two-step process to localize a drug-activating enzyme and then administer a selectively activated prodrug to minimize off-target effects. Adapted to the context of bacterial resistance, the research idea is to develop a two-component therapeutic strategy. First, a 'priming' agent (e.g., a sub-inhibitory beta-lactam) is used to further induce or stabilize AmpC beta-lactamase hyperexpression specifically in resistant pathogens at the infection site. Second, a novel prodrug is administered that is a substrate for AmpC and is cleaved into a potent, membrane-disrupting cytotoxin (e.g., a modified antimicrobial peptide or a novel bactericidal compound), thereby turning the resistance mechanism into a lethal vulnerability for the targeted bacteria while sparing host cells and commensal flora with low AmpC activity.

ideas.title: Using CBDE as a pragmatic reference to harmonize heterogeneous AmpC treatment data for meta-analysis
ideas.description: This research investigates whether Clinical/Bacteriological Disease Eradication (CBDE), a simpler and more accessible outcome measure than Bone Mineral Density (BMD) or complex microbiological endpoints, can be repurposed as a pragmatic reference standard to harmonize heterogeneous observational data on AmpC beta-lactamase-producing organism treatments. By applying advanced meta-analytic techniques, such as network meta-regression and bias-adjustment models, the study will use CBDE to calibrate across studies with divergent designs, treatment protocols, and outcome assessments. The goal is to create an adjusted evidence synthesis that extracts a coherent signal on comparative antibiotic effectiveness from noisy, real-world data where traditional meta-analysis fails. This approach inverts the typical hierarchy of evidence by using a pragmatic outcome to enable rigorous inference from inherently heterogeneous observational sources.

ideas.title: Rapid, point-of-care antifungal susceptibility phenotyping using isothermal amplification of resistance marker transcripts
ideas.description: This research fuses the simplicity of broth disk elution for antifungal susceptibility testing with the speed and specificity of isothermal amplification assays. The core concept is to use a caspofungin-impregnated disk to create a drug gradient in a small volume of broth containing the fungal pathogen; after a short incubation, the broth is directly analyzed using a CRISPR-based or recombinase polymerase amplification (RPA) assay that targets upregulated transcripts of key echinocandin resistance genes, like FKS1 hotspot mutations in Candida. This synergy allows for a same-shift answer, providing not just a growth/no-growth phenotypic result but also a specific molecular confirmation of the resistance mechanism, all from a single, low-complexity test suitable for resource-limited or point-of-care settings.

ideas.title: High-Resolution Gradient MIC with Sub-Population Analysis to Replace Discrete Tubes
ideas.description: This research develops an integrated system that combines a continuous antibiotic gradient with sensitive, automated sub-population detection. It uses microfluidic channels or agar gradients to create a precise concentration field, while time-lapse imaging or endpoint flow cytometry analyzes individual bacterial cells or micro-colonies for growth. Machine learning algorithms then map growth patterns to identify not just the primary MIC, but also the presence, proportion, and MIC of any heteroresistant or minority sub-populations. This approach aims to provide superior accuracy and richer data than four discrete tubes, without sacrificing the critical ability to detect clinically significant resistance sub-types.

ideas.title: Applying Pharmaceutical Reference Standard Calibration to Antibiotic Disk Potency Verification
ideas.description: This research idea challenges the assumption of negligible colistin disk potency variability in CBDE tests by drawing an analogy from pharmaceutical analytical chemistry. In that field, the problem of variable raw material potency is solved by using certified chemical reference standards (CRS) to calibrate analytical methods. The adapted idea proposes to apply this solution by obtaining a pharmacopeial-grade colistin reference standard. A standardized agar diffusion assay would be calibrated using this CRS to create a definitive dose-response (zone size) curve. Disks from various commercial lots and manufacturers would then be tested in this calibrated system. Their resulting zone sizes would be compared back to the standard curve to calculate their effective delivered potency, objectively quantifying lot-to-lot variability and its potential impact on clinical breakpoint interpretation and susceptibility categorization.

ideas.title: Investigate CBDE performance for mcr-1-positive isolates using a diagnostic interference framework
ideas.description: This research idea applies a problem-solving approach from clinical chemistry, where diagnostic test interference is systematically deconstructed. The analogous problem is a specific immunoassay giving false results due to a known interfering antibody or metabolite, solved by spiking studies to confirm and characterize the interferent. Adapted to the microbiology context, the idea is to investigate why CBDE produces errors for mcr-1-positive E. coli by treating the MCR-1 enzyme or its biochemical products as potential 'interferents' in the detection system. The adapted solution involves controlled in vitro spiking experiments: introducing purified MCR-1 enzyme, its lipid A modification product (phosphoethanolamine), or plasmid extracts from mcr-1-positive isolates into CBDE reaction mixtures with known mcr-1-negative control strains. The resultant changes in test metrics (e.g., hydrolysis rates, fluorescence signals) will be analyzed to determine if the error is caused by direct enzymatic interference with the CBDE substrate, competition for the detection signal, or a non-specific matrix effect stemming from the modified bacterial membrane, thereby pinpointing the mechanism of the performance failure.

ideas.title: Integrated Genotypic-Phenotypic Microfluidic System for Rapid, Specific Detection of Resistant Pathogens
ideas.description: This system combines rapid, multiplexed nucleic acid detection (using isothermal amplification and CRISPR) with a brief, downstream phenotypic confirmation in an integrated microfluidic cartridge. The first stage screens for a panel of plasmid-mediated resistance genes directly from a urine or blood sample. Any positive genetic signal automatically initiates a second stage, where the sample is exposed to a relevant antibiotic in a nanoliter culture chamber. Optical sensors monitor for early, pathogen-specific growth inhibition over 30-60 minutes, confirming that the detected gene is expressed in a viable, likely pathogenic bacterium. This closed-loop approach ensures results are clinically actionable by distinguishing true resistance in pathogens from bystander carriage.

ideas.title: Apply predator-prey signaling game theory to AmpC expression dynamics
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from behavioral ecology—specifically, game theory models of predator-prey interactions and costly signaling (e.g., the Hawk-Dove game)—to the problem of antibiotic resistance spread. In ecology, such models solve how animals optimize costly defensive behaviors based on environmental threats and the actions of their peers. The analogous solution is adapted by framing antibiotics as the selective 'predator' pressure, inducible AmpC beta-lactamase production as a metabolically costly 'defensive signal' for the bacterium, and constitutive non-expression as a low-cost 'hiding' strategy. The adapted research idea uses this framework to model the population dynamics of AmpC expression, predicting how treatment regimens (frequency and intensity) and bacterial population structure (density, mixing) influence the evolutionary game between resistant and susceptible strains, ultimately forecasting the conditions for the spread or containment of resistance.

ideas.title: Multi-scale spatial agent-based model of AmpC resistance integrating evolutionary game theory with population genomics and ecology
ideas.description: This approach combines spatial evolutionary game theory, agent-based modeling, and genomic data to simulate the emergence and spread of AmpC-mediated resistance. Bacterial agents, endowed with realistic regulatory circuits for AmpC expression and associated fitness costs, interact on a spatially explicit grid representing a host tissue or biofilm, where antibiotic and nutrient gradients create locally varying evolutionary games. The model scales from within-host microenvironments to between-host transmission networks, enabling prediction of resistance trajectories under realistic treatment regimens and public health interventions. Validation uses longitudinal genomic and phenotypic data from clinical isolates to iteratively refine agent rules and spatial parameters.

ideas.title: Inverting the Reference Standard: Adapting the 'Gold Standard' Shift from Clinical Microbiology to Bone Densitometry
ideas.description: This research idea applies an analogy from clinical microbiology, where the diagnostic 'gold standard' has been pragmatically shifted in resource-limited settings from complex lab-based methods to simpler, accessible alternatives. The analogous solution involved validating the simpler test's performance and clinical utility within the specific context, making it the de facto reference. Adapted to bone densitometry, the research would investigate if CBDE (a simpler, potentially more accessible technology) could be validated as the primary reference standard for well-defined use cases—such as monitoring osteoporosis therapy in low-risk postmenopausal women in primary care or community health settings—instead of the more complex and costly BMD. The study would establish diagnostic performance benchmarks, clinical decision concordance with expert review, and cost-effectiveness, aiming to create a new, context-appropriate standard of care.

ideas.title: A Global Genomic Weather Map for Antimicrobial Resistance
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from meteorology's global observing network to the challenge of scaling antimicrobial resistance surveillance. The analogous field is Meteorology and Climate Science, specifically the World Meteorological Organization's (WMO) Integrated Global Observing System (WIGOS), which synthesizes disparate global data into a unified, real-time model of atmospheric conditions. The adapted research idea is to create a 'Global Genomic Weather Map for Antimicrobial Resistance' by establishing a network of sentinel labs that use standardized protocols to submit genomic data on AmpC-producing Enterobacteriaceae to a central, cloud-based analytical platform. This platform would employ shared bioinformatic pipelines and phylogenetic models to integrate the data, enabling the real-time visualization, tracking, and predictive modeling of resistant bacterial lineage dissemination across the globe, much as meteorologists track weather systems and storms.

ideas.title: Single-Cell Transcriptional Bursting Analysis of AmpC Induction via Microfluidics and MS2-GFP Tagging
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from eukaryotic gene regulation—live-cell imaging of transcriptional bursts using the MS2-GFP RNA tagging system—to the problem of studying AmpC expression heterogeneity in single bacteria. In the analogous field, this technique has been crucial for quantifying stochastic gene activation in real time within developing embryos. By engineering an E. coli strain with an MS2-tagged ampC allele and using microfluidic traps (e.g., a mother machine) for long-term, high-resolution imaging, we can adapt this method to directly visualize and measure the dynamics of individual ampC transcription events in single cells. This will reveal the precise induction kinetics, transcriptional noise, and cell-to-cell variability that underlie heterogeneous beta-lactamase expression and the emergence of antibiotic resistance, moving beyond population averages to a single-allele, single-cell understanding of this critical resistance mechanism.

ideas.title: Phenotype-Adaptive CBDE Testing Framework for Personalized Metabolic Assessment
ideas.description: This research develops a flexible, phenotype-driven framework for the Cannabinoid Bio-Detoxification Enzyme (CBDE) test, moving beyond a single standardized protocol. The system first classifies patients into metabolic phenotypes using a minimal set of pre-test clinical variables. It then automatically selects and executes a corresponding optimized analytical protocol from a validated library, tailoring the assay to the individual's predicted enzymatic kinetics. This approach ensures result accuracy across diverse populations and enhances clinical relevance by directly linking the lab value to a patient-specific metabolic context.

ideas.title: Causal deconstruction of single-cell AmpC induction via integrated trigger and regulator quantification
ideas.description: This study moves beyond observing heterogeneous expression to causally link induction triggers to the regulatory response in individual bacteria. We combine microfluidic chemostats with multiplexed single-molecule RNA FISH to quantify the intracellular presence of specific putative inducing signals (e.g., muropeptide biosynthesis transcripts) alongside the key regulator ampR mRNA. This molecular snapshot is correlated in real time with a robust, single-cell-calibrated fluorescent reporter for AmpC β-lactamase activity under precisely controlled antibiotic gradients. The integrated system thereby tests specific hypotheses about which molecular events are necessary and sufficient for induction in single cells, transforming descriptive heterogeneity maps into a mechanistic understanding of the induction decision circuit.

ideas.title: Applying Single-Cell Genomics Principles to Detect Colistin Heteroresistance
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that visual turbidity reading is adequate to detect colistin heteroresistance by drawing an analogy from cancer biology. In oncology, bulk tumor analysis failed to detect rare, resistant subclones, a problem solved by single-cell genomics. We adapt this solution by proposing a shift from bulk culture turbidity metrics to single-cell resolution analysis of bacterial populations. The adapted research idea is to develop or apply high-throughput single-cell techniques (like microfluidics or time-lapse imaging) to directly identify, quantify, and characterize the colistin-resistant subpopulations that constitute heteroresistance, thereby providing a more accurate and predictive detection method.

The study then tests whether this validated model can reliably serve as a reference standard within a strictly defined, practical context, such as monitoring treatment response in a single clinic. This approach moves from a speculative inversion to an evidence-based, context-limited proposal for reference standard utility.

ideas.title: Applying Ecological Succession Principles to Decolonize Intestinal AmpC Reservoirs
ideas.description: This research adapts the ecological succession model from Restoration Ecology to develop a decolonization regimen for intestinal AmpC producers. The analogous solution involves staged intervention to restore a healthy ecosystem, not just eliminate an invader. The adapted idea investigates a multi-phase strategy: initial targeted suppression of AmpC producers, followed by sequential introduction of microbial consortia or substrates that progressively modify the intestinal niche to be unfavorable for resistant bacteria, culminating in the establishment of a diverse, protective resident microbiota that prevents resurgence, thereby scaling treatment from acute infection management to long-term carriage prevention.

ideas.title: Adapting Environmental Biosensor Architecture for Multiplexed Plasmid Detection
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from environmental monitoring biosensing to the problem of detecting plasmid-mediated ampC genes. The analogous field is Environmental Monitoring and Biosensing, where the solution involves field-deployable, paper-based microfluidic biosensors using isothermal amplification coupled with colorimetric or fluorescent nanoparticle reporters for multiplexed detection of contaminants. We adapt this by designing a point-of-care device that uses a paper-based microfluidic chip architecture. The chip integrates sample lysis, recombinase polymerase amplification (RPA) of plasmid-borne ampC genes, and subsequent CRISPR-Cas12a detection. Different CRISPR RNAs (crRNAs) target specific ampC gene families, and their activation cleaves distinct, color-coded quantum dot reporters deposited in separate detection zones. This allows for visual, multiplexed identification of key ampC genotypes (e.g., CIT, FOX, EBC) directly from a bacterial colony, providing a rapid, low-cost diagnostic tool for infection control.

ideas.title: Applying Climate Model Ensemble Techniques to AmpC Treatment Meta-Analysis
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that extreme heterogeneity in observational AmpC treatment data precludes useful inference by drawing an analogy from climate science. In that field, predictions are synthesized from ensembles of structurally different global climate models (GCMs) using techniques like emergent constraints to produce robust, uncertainty-quantified projections. The adapted solution applies this ensemble framework to meta-analysis. Heterogeneous treatment studies are treated as distinct models in an ensemble. Advanced Bayesian hierarchical modeling and pattern recognition (e.g., seeking dose-response or biomarker-response relationships that emerge across studies) are used to dynamically weight and constrain this ensemble. This transforms heterogeneity from a confounding factor into a source of information, allowing for the extraction of a more reliable signal on treatment efficacy and the explicit quantification of how assumptions in original studies influence the overall inference.

ideas.title: Adapt Microfluidics Scaling Principles to Reduce Broth Volume Requirements
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that standard 10-ml broth volumes are necessary for a microbial culture method. By drawing an analogy from microfluidics and lab-on-a-chip technology—where assays are routinely miniaturized from milliliter to microliter scales—we can adapt their scaling principles. The analogous solution involves precise control of fluid dynamics and enhanced detection for small volumes. Adapted to this context, the research idea is to design a specialized culture vessel or insert that maintains critical growth parameters (like gas exchange and mixing) in significantly smaller broth volumes (1-5ml). This would involve surface treatments to manage cell behavior and the integration of enhanced, miniaturized detection techniques to reliably monitor the assay endpoint, thereby reducing reagent use and waste while validating method performance.

ideas.title: Objective digital readout of colorimetric CBDE endpoints via standardized image analysis
ideas.description: This redesigned system incorporates a colorimetric growth indicator into the CBDE tube but pairs it with a standardized digital readout protocol to eliminate visual subjectivity. The protocol uses a fixed lighting setup and a reference color card to capture a consistent image of the tube endpoint, typically with a smartphone. A dedicated application then analyzes the specific hue and saturation values against calibrated thresholds to determine the MIC or breakpoint automatically. This approach combines the biochemical clarity of a color change with the objectivity and reproducibility of a digital measurement.

ideas.title: Adapting Litmus Paper's Color Gradient for Microbial Growth Endpoints
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from the use of litmus paper in analytical chemistry, which provides a clear visual endpoint via a sharp color change at a specific pH threshold, this idea adapts that principle for CBDE tubes. The analogous solution uses a dye mixture for a distinct, threshold-based color transition. The adapted research idea is to develop a composite colorimetric indicator system for CBDE tubes that uses a mixture of pH-sensitive and redox-sensitive dyes. Microbial growth would metabolically shift the environment, triggering a coordinated, multi-hue color transition (e.g., from green to purple) only after a threshold of metabolic activity is reached, creating a stark, unambiguous visual endpoint for growth detection, thereby improving the accuracy and ease of visual reading.
